questions.text: Will the new card described in the stem be able to overcome the limitations of the first card provided to Ms. Rubin? Give your opinion with arguments.
questions.answer:
Data: The first card required a balance (Debit Card), while the new one works "without a balance" and is suitable for "going abroad."
Analysis: A Credit Card provides a loan limit and is widely accepted internationally, unlike a standard local Debit Card.
Evaluation: The Credit Card solves the problem of balance dependency and currency limitations.
Decision: Yes, the new 'Credit Card' successfully overcomes the limitations of the 'Debit Card' by providing emergency credit and international transaction capabilities.

fullCQData.stem: Rumi and Jami work at an organization in Motijheel, Dhaka. They live in Dhaka and operate accounts at the Motijheel branch of Swarnachura Bank. The two colleagues went to Cox's Bazar for a trip. For transaction convenience, Jami took bank checks and Rumi took a plastic card issued against his bank account. Jami completed all expenses by withdrawing money via check from the Cox's Bazar branch of Swarnachura Bank, and Rumi met his expenses by withdrawing money from an ATM.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is One-Stop Service?
questions.answer: One-Stop Service is a banking arrangement where a customer can receive all their required banking services (like deposits, withdrawals, and balance inquiries) from a single counter or desk.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is electronic banking important for ensuring customer service?
questions.answer: E-banking is important because it provides 24/7 access to funds, reduces the time spent in bank queues, allows for instant bill payments, and ensures accuracy and transparency in financial transactions.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What banking facility does Jami receive in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Jami used a "check" at the "Cox's Bazar branch" while his account is in the "Motijheel branch."
Analysis: The facility that allows a customer to access their account from any branch of the same bank network is called 'Any Branch Banking' or 'Online Banking'.
Conclusion: Jami is receiving 'Online/Core Banking' facilities.

questions.label: d
questions.text: The capacity of Rumi's card is linked to the amount of money deposited in his bank—Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: Rumi uses a plastic card to withdraw his own money from an ATM.
Analysis: A card that only allows transactions within the limit of the actual balance in a savings/current account is a Debit Card.
Evaluation: Unlike a Credit Card, a Debit Card holder cannot spend more than what they have deposited.
Decision: The statement is true; Rumi is using a 'Debit Card', meaning his purchasing power is strictly limited to his actual bank balance.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Arju and Mr. Omar have separate bank accounts. The bank gave them two plastic cards. They went shopping at a mall. They were able to shop using the plastic cards. In this case, Arju had money in his bank account, but Omar did not have any money in his bank account.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Point of Sale (POS) service?
questions.answer: Point of Sale (POS) service is a system where a customer makes a payment for goods or services at a retail location using a plastic card through an electronic terminal.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by Automated Clearing House? Explain.
questions.answer: Automated Clearing House (ACH) is an electronic network for financial transactions that processes large volumes of credit and debit batches automatically, simplifying the settlement of funds between different banks.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which plastic card of the bank was Mr. Arju able to shop? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Arju had "money in his bank account" and used a card to shop.
Analysis: A card that allows a user to pay for goods by deducting the amount from their existing bank balance is a Debit Card.
Conclusion: Mr. Arju was able to shop using a 'Debit Card'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Provide a comparative analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of the two plastic cards mentioned in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Arju used a Debit Card; Omar used a Credit Card (no balance required).
Analysis: Debit Card Advantages: No interest, promotes budget control. Disadvantages: Requires balance. Credit Card Advantages: Emergency loan, "buy now pay later." Disadvantages: Interest charges, risk of debt trap.
Evaluation: Debit cards are safer for saving; Credit cards are better for financial crises.
Decision: Both cards enhance liquidity, but the 'Credit Card' offers more flexibility at the cost of potential interest, while the 'Debit Card' is cost-free but limited by the user's savings.

mcqData.text: The primary function of insurance is-
mcqData.explanation: Insurance primarily provides financial certainty or assurance against potential loss.

mcqData.text: Which is a way to mitigate business risk?
mcqData.explanation: Insurance is a formal mechanism to transfer or mitigate specific business risks.

mcqData.text: Which is an essential element of insurance?
mcqData.explanation: An "insurable interest" is the legal prerequisite for any valid insurance contract.

mcqData.text: Stem: A person in London had a coffee shop where sailors frequented; it became a hub for marine insurance. Q: What is his name?
mcqData.explanation: Edward Lloyd's Coffee House in London is the historical birthplace of Lloyd's of London, the insurance market.

mcqData.text:
Stem: (Same as above) Q: Significance of the insurance in his shop:

(i) Financial security
(ii) Trade expansion
(iii) Risk reduction
mcqData.explanation: Insurance provides security, reduces risk, and encourages international trade.

mcqData.text: Through which did the insurance business originate?
mcqData.explanation: Marine insurance is historically the oldest form of insurance.

mcqData.text: The regulatory body for insurance in Bangladesh is?
mcqData.explanation: IDRA (Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority) regulates the insurance sector in Bangladesh.

mcqData.text:
Indemnity contracts are-

(i) Fire
(ii) Life
(iii) Marine
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance is a "benefit" contract, whereas Fire and Marine are indemnity contracts.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Nasir rents a house and wants to insure it in his own name, but the company refuses. Q: What insurance did he want?
mcqData.explanation: Since it relates to a house, it is Property Insurance.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Reason for refusal by the company?
mcqData.explanation: A tenant generally lacks an "insurable interest" in the ownership of the building unless specified.

mcqData.text:
General elements of insurance contracts:

(i) Voluntary
(ii) Proposal & Acceptance
(iii) Proximate cause
mcqData.explanation: "Proximate cause" is a specific insurance principle, not a general contract element like the first two.

mcqData.text: What is the short form of the insurance regulator in Bangladesh?
mcqData.explanation: IDRA is the officially recognized abbreviation.

mcqData.text: Which insurance helps increase invisible income in foreign trade?
mcqData.explanation: Marine insurance services are critical for international trade flow.

mcqData.text:
Indemnity contracts are-

(i) Life
(ii) Marine
(iii) Fire
mcqData.explanation: Marine and Fire insurance strictly follow the principle of indemnity.

mcqData.text: Stem: X has stored seasonal goods near a chemical factory. Q: Which policy should he take?
mcqData.explanation: Given the fire hazard from the nearby factory, Fire Insurance is necessary.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Which principle will the company follow?
mcqData.explanation: The fundamental basis of starting any insurance policy is the existence of an "Insurable interest."

mcqData.text: Legal element of insurance contract?
mcqData.explanation: Like any contract, it requires "lawful consideration" (the premium paid for the cover).

mcqData.text: Under which principle can property be insured by multiple insurers?
mcqData.explanation: Pro-rata contribution ensures multiple insurers share the loss proportionately.

mcqData.text: The oldest insurance is?
mcqData.explanation: Marine insurance dates back to ancient trade routes.

mcqData.text: Which is covered under General Insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Crop insurance is a form of general insurance, whereas others are more social/life-based.

mcqData.text: Insurance is-
mcqData.explanation: Insurance is fundamentally a financial defense mechanism against risk.

mcqData.text: Stem: Exporter sends goods by ship and includes insurance costs. Q: Which insurance is done?
mcqData.explanation: Marine insurance covers goods in transit by sea.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: What is the subject matter of the insurance?
mcqData.explanation: The goods themselves constitute the subject matter of the policy here.

mcqData.text: Where is there no insurable interest?
mcqData.explanation: A debtor does not have an insurable interest in the creditor's life.

mcqData.text: The objective of insurance is-
mcqData.explanation: Insurance aims to reduce the financial impact/severity of risks.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Anil has rented a house for a long time. He wanted to insure it in his own name, but the insurance company refused. Q: What type of insurance did he want?
mcqData.explanation: Since the subject matter is a building/house, it is classified as Property Insurance.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Reason for refusal?
mcqData.explanation: A tenant usually lacks an insurable interest in the ownership of the building structure itself.

mcqData.text: The financial interest of the policyholder in the insured subject matter is called?
mcqData.explanation: Insurable Interest is the legal requirement for any valid insurance policy.

mcqData.text: Which is a general element of an insurance contract?
mcqData.explanation: "Proposal & Acceptance" are general elements of any legal contract, while others are specific insurance principles.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Akash insured his car for 10 lakhs. It was totally damaged, and the company compensated him but took ownership of the salvage. Q: What insurance was this?
mcqData.explanation: Vehicles are categorized under Property Insurance.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Characteristics of the policy that forced Akash to give up the car?
options: Property totally damaged, full compensation paid, salvage ownership transferred
mcqData.explanation: This reflects the Principle of Subrogation: once compensated fully, the insurer takes the salvaged property.

mcqData.text: In which case is insurable interest present?
mcqData.explanation: A creditor has an insurable interest in a debtor's life regarding the loan amount.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Ahmed rents a house and wants to insure it in his own name, but the company refuses. Q: What insurance did he want?
mcqData.explanation: Building insurance falls under Property Insurance.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Reason for rejection?
mcqData.explanation: The rejection is based on the lack of insurable interest by the tenant.

mcqData.text: Subrogation is NOT applicable in which insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance is not a contract of indemnity; thus, subrogation does not apply.

mcqData.text: What is the main objective of insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Insurance aims to distribute the risk among many participants.

mcqData.text: Insurance laws in Bangladesh are currently managed under which year's act?
mcqData.explanation: The Insurance Act, 2010 is the prevailing legislation in Bangladesh.

mcqData.text:
General Insurance includes:

(i) Fire
(ii) Machinery
(iii) Pension
mcqData.explanation: Pension insurance is typically categorized under Life/Social insurance.

mcqData.text: What is the possibility of financial loss called?
mcqData.explanation: Risk is defined as the uncertainty or possibility of loss.

mcqData.text:
Important for national development:

(i) Risk assessment
(ii) Saving habit
(iii) National saving rate

shortCQData.question: What is a Mortality Table?
shortCQData.answer: A Mortality Table is a statistical table that shows the probability of death for persons at each age, used by life insurance companies to calculate premiums.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by the Principle of Indemnity?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Indemnity states that insurance is meant to restore the insured to their previous financial position after a loss, ensuring they do not make a profit from the disaster.

shortCQData.question: What is Double Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Double Insurance is a situation where the same subject matter or risk is insured with two or more insurance companies for the same period.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by Financial Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Financial Risk refers to the possibility of monetary loss resulting from an event, where the impact can be measured and expressed in terms of money.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurable Interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable Interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a loss from its damage.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by the Principle of Utmost Good Faith?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Utmost Good Faith implies that both the insurer and the insured must voluntarily disclose all relevant and material facts truthfully when entering an insurance contract.

shortCQData.question: What is Surrender Value?
shortCQData.answer: Surrender Value is the amount an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they voluntarily terminate the policy before its maturity or before the insured event occurs.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by a Mortality Table?
shortCQData.answer: A Mortality Table is a statistical chart showing the probability of death at different ages, which insurers use to determine life insurance premiums and risk levels.

shortCQData.question: What is Surrender Value?
shortCQData.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount offered by an insurance company to the policyholder upon the cancellation of a life insurance policy before it reaches maturity.

shortCQData.question: Insurance is a system of risk distribution—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a system of risk distribution because it collects small premiums from a large number of people exposed to similar risks to form a fund, which is then used to compensate the few who actually suffer a loss.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurable Interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable Interest is the legal right to insure some property or life, where the person would suffer a financial loss if the property is damaged or the person dies.

shortCQData.question: How is risk-related hindrance removed? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Risk-related hindrance is removed through insurance, where the financial burden of a potential loss is transferred from an individual or business to an insurance company in exchange for a premium.

shortCQData.question: What is risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome resulting from an action or an event, specifically the uncertainty regarding financial loss.

shortCQData.question: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they arise from natural disasters (like earthquakes or floods) or macro-economic shifts (like sudden inflation or political instability) that individuals or businesses cannot predict or prevent through internal measures.

shortCQData.question: What is Speculative Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Speculative risk is a type of risk that offers the possibility of either a gain or a loss, such as investing in the stock market or a new business venture.

shortCQData.question: How does insurance distribute risk? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance distributes risk by pooling the premiums of many individuals exposed to similar risks. This collective fund is then used to pay for the losses of the few who actually suffer an unfortunate event, effectively spreading the financial burden across the group.

shortCQData.question: What is the Principle of Indemnity?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Indemnity is a rule in insurance stating that the insurer will pay only the actual amount of the loss suffered, ensuring the insured does not profit from the insurance.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because both the insurer and the insured are legally bound to disclose all material facts and information truthfully to ensure a fair assessment of risk.

shortCQData.question: What is insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a legal contract where one party (the insurer) agrees to compensate another party (the insured) for a specific loss or damage in exchange for a fee called a premium.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest means the legal right of the insured to insure an object or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a contract of indemnity (except for life insurance) where the insurer promises to make good the loss of the insured in exchange for a premium.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance important for the expansion of international trade? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is vital for international trade because it covers high-risk maritime perils (like shipwrecks or piracy), providing financial security to exporters and importers, which encourages them to engage in large-scale global commerce.

shortCQData.question: What is Fundamental Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire economy or a large number of people simultaneously, such as war, inflation, or natural disasters like floods.

shortCQData.question: What is the nearest cause of loss called? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The nearest cause of loss is called 'Causa Proxima' (Proximate Cause). It means that the insurer only pays for a loss if it was directly caused by a peril specifically covered in the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full compensation for a loss, they acquire all legal rights and ownership of the damaged property (salvage) from the insured.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a joint system of risk distribution? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a joint system of risk distribution because it collects small premiums from many people exposed to similar risks to create a common fund. This fund is then used to compensate the few who actually suffer a loss, effectively spreading the burden.

shortCQData.question: What is an Insurance Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the specific amount of money paid periodically by the insured to the insurance company in exchange for the risk coverage provided by the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurable interest important in the insurance business? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is important because it prevents insurance from becoming a form of gambling. It ensures that the person taking the policy has a genuine financial relationship with the subject and will actually suffer a loss if damage occurs.

shortCQData.question: What is risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility or uncertainty of a financial loss or an unfavorable outcome resulting from a specific event.

shortCQData.question: What do you mean by Surrender Value?
shortCQData.answer: Surrender value is the cash amount that an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate a life insurance policy before its maturity date.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the fixed amount of money that the insured pays to the insurance company periodically in exchange for the risk coverage provided.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because both the insurer and the insured are legally bound to disclose all relevant and material facts truthfully to ensure a fair assessment of the risk.

shortCQData.question: What is risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome, specifically the uncertainty regarding a potential financial loss.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right to insure an object or life because the insured has a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged or destroyed.

shortCQData.question: What is uncertainty?
shortCQData.answer: Uncertainty is a state where the outcome of an event is unknown and cannot be accurately predicted or measured.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because it relies on the voluntary and honest disclosure of all material facts by both the insurer and the insured to maintain the validity of the contract.

shortCQData.question: Who is the Insurer?
shortCQData.answer: The insurer is the party or company that agrees to provide compensation for a specific loss or damage in exchange for a premium.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of a person to insure a subject matter because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a direct monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the amount paid periodically or in a lump sum by the policyholder to the insurer for covering a specific risk.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because both parties must voluntarily and honestly disclose all material facts related to the risk to ensure the contract is fair and valid.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a legal agreement where an insurer promises to compensate the insured for a specific loss in exchange for a premium.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a system of risk distribution?
shortCQData.answer: It is called a system of risk distribution because it pools the small premiums of many people to pay for the large losses of a few, effectively spreading the financial burden across the group.

shortCQData.question: What is risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable outcome or financial loss arising from an event whose occurrence is uncertain.

shortCQData.question: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is not gambling because its purpose is to protect against a potential loss and restore the insured to their previous position (Indemnity), whereas gambling is designed to create a profit or gain based purely on chance.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the periodic payment made by the insured to the insurer in exchange for taking on the specified risk.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk refers to the uncertainty regarding a loss; it is the measurable chance that an actual outcome will differ from an expected outcome.

shortCQData.question: What is Re-insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Re-insurance is a process where an insurance company transfers a portion of its own risk to another insurance company to protect itself from large losses.

shortCQData.question: How is Double Insurance done? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Double Insurance is done when a person insures the same subject matter (property or life) with two or more different insurance companies for the same period and risk.

shortCQData.question: What is Fundamental Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire society or a large number of people at once, such as war, inflation, or natural disasters.

shortCQData.question: Insurance is not a wager or gambling—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is not gambling because it is meant to provide financial protection against an existing risk and restore the insured to their previous position (Indemnity), whereas gambling creates a new risk for the purpose of making a profit.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the consideration or fee paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the promise to cover a specific risk.

shortCQData.question: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because it relies on the honest disclosure of all material facts by both parties; if any truth is hidden, the contract becomes legally invalid.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurable Interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure an object or life because they would suffer a financial loss if that object or life were harmed.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because the insurer cannot physically inspect every minor detail of the risk and must rely on the truthful representation of facts provided by the insured.

shortCQData.question: What is an Insurance Contract?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance contract is a legal agreement where the insurer, in exchange for a premium, promises to compensate the insured for losses caused by specified risks or events.

shortCQData.question: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of indemnity? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because it aims to compensate the insured only for the actual loss suffered, ensuring they are returned to their previous financial position without making any profit from the disaster.

shortCQData.question: What is Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility or uncertainty of a financial loss or an unfavorable outcome resulting from an unexpected event.

shortCQData.question: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is not gambling because it is meant to provide protection against an existing potential loss (Indemnity), whereas gambling creates a new risk for the sole purpose of making a profit based on chance.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: A premium is the consideration or fee paid by the insured to the insurance company in exchange for the financial protection provided by the policy.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of a person to insure someone or something because they benefit from its existence and would suffer a financial loss upon its death or destruction.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the amount of money paid by the insured to the insurance company periodically or in a lump sum in exchange for risk coverage.

shortCQData.question: Is an insurance contract possible without insurable interest? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: No, an insurance contract is not legally valid without insurable interest. It prevents insurance from becoming gambling and ensures that the insured has a genuine financial stake in the subject matter.

shortCQData.question: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full claim for a loss, they acquire all legal rights and ownership of the remaining damaged property (salvage).

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is Ideal Moral Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Ideal Moral Risk refers to a situation where the insured person is exceptionally honest, disciplined, and takes more care than a regular person to prevent any loss or damage to the insured property.

shortCQData.question: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: An insurance contract is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because it relies on the voluntary and honest disclosure of all material facts by both parties; if any truth is hidden, the contract becomes legally void.

shortCQData.question: What is Fundamental Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire economy or a large group of people simultaneously, such as war, massive floods, or sudden inflation.

shortCQData.question: What is the nearest cause of loss called? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The nearest cause of loss is called 'Causa Proxima' (Proximate Cause). It means that for a claim to be valid, the loss must be directly caused by a peril that is explicitly covered in the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: What is Surrender Value?
shortCQData.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount that a life insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate the policy before its maturity or before the insured event occurs.

shortCQData.question: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they originate from natural forces (earthquakes, cyclones) or systemic economic factors (market crashes) that an individual cannot prevent through their own actions or precautions.

shortCQData.question: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full compensation for a loss to the insured, all legal rights and ownership of the remaining damaged property (salvage) pass to the insurer.

shortCQData.question: Why is there no insurance without a contract?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a legal relationship between two parties where risks are transferred for a consideration (premium). Since it involves legal rights, obligations, and enforceability in court, it must be based on a formal contract.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurable Interest?
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to take out insurance because they have a financial relationship with the subject matter and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: For which insurance is the principle of subrogation not applicable? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Subrogation is not applicable to Life Insurance. This is because human life is invaluable and cannot be measured in money, so life insurance is a 'Contract of Certainty,' not a 'Contract of Indemnity.'

shortCQData.question: What is Double Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Double Insurance occurs when a person insures the same subject matter or risk with two or more different insurance companies for the same period.

shortCQData.question: What do you mean by the Principle of Contribution? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a person has multiple insurance policies for the same risk, all insurers will share the loss proportionately so the insured only receives the actual loss amount.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a legal agreement where an insurer, in exchange for a premium, promises to compensate the insured for a specific loss or damage.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurance called a system of risk distribution?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is called a risk distribution system because it collects small premiums from many people exposed to similar risks to create a fund, which is then used to pay for the losses of the few who actually suffer.

shortCQData.question: What is risk?
shortCQData.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome resulting from an action or an event, specifically the uncertainty regarding financial loss.

shortCQData.question: What is meant by insurable interest? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is Surrender Value?
shortCQData.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount that an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate a life insurance policy before its maturity.

shortCQData.question: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they arise from natural forces (earthquakes, cyclones) or macro-economic shifts that individuals cannot prevent through their own actions.

shortCQData.question: What is Liability Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Liability Insurance is a type of insurance that provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

shortCQData.question: Trust is the capital of an insurance contract—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called the capital of insurance because the contract is based on 'Utmost Good Faith' (Uberrimae Fidei). Since the insurer cannot see every detail of the risk, they rely on the insured's honest disclosure; any lie makes the contract worthless.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the consideration or price paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the risk coverage provided by the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: Insurance is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because its purpose is to compensate the insured for the actual loss suffered and restore them to their pre-loss financial position, without allowing any profit.

shortCQData.question: What is Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is a legal contract where the insurer agrees to compensate the insured for specific losses in return for a premium.

shortCQData.question: What do you mean by insurable interest? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right to insure an object or life because the insured has a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is a Premium?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance premium is the specific amount of money paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the promise to provide financial coverage against a risk.

shortCQData.question: Insurance contract is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because its primary goal is to compensate the insured for the actual loss suffered and return them to their original financial position without allowing any profit.

shortCQData.question: What is Moral Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Moral risk refers to the possibility of a loss occurring due to the dishonesty, bad character, or intentional negligence of the insured person to gain from the insurance.

shortCQData.question: What do you mean by the Principle of Contribution? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a person has multiple insurance policies for the same property and risk, the insurers will share the actual loss proportionately so that the insured does not receive more than the actual loss.

shortCQData.question: What is an Insurance Contract?
shortCQData.answer: An insurance contract is a legal agreement where an insurer agrees to provide financial protection or compensation against specific risks in exchange for a premium payment.

shortCQData.question: Explain the principle of Proximate Cause in insurance.
shortCQData.answer: The principle of 'Proximate Cause' (Causa Proxima) states that the insurer is only liable to pay if the loss was directly and immediately caused by a peril that is specifically covered in the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: What is Liability Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Liability insurance is a type of insurance that provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

shortCQData.question: Explain the Principle of Contribution in insurance.
shortCQData.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a property is insured with multiple companies for the same risk, all insurers will share the loss proportionately so that the insured only receives the actual loss amount and does not profit.

shortCQData.question: What is Fundamental Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that is impersonal in origin and affects a large group of people or the entire economy, such as war, natural disasters, or inflation.

shortCQData.question: Is it mandatory for an insurance contract to be in writing? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Yes, an insurance contract should be in writing because it serves as legal evidence (the policy) in case of disputes, ensures all terms and conditions are clearly defined, and is required by insurance laws for enforceability.

shortCQData.question: What is Liability Insurance?
shortCQData.answer: Liability Insurance provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

shortCQData.question: What role does the regulatory body play in developing the insurance business in Bangladesh? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: The 'Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority' (IDRA) ensures transparency, protects policyholders' rights, monitors company solvency, and sets guidelines to build public trust and stabilize the market.

shortCQData.question: What is Fundamental Risk?
shortCQData.answer: Fundamental risk is a risk that affects the entire society or a large group of people simultaneously, such as natural disasters or national economic shifts.

shortCQData.question: Why is insurable interest important in the insurance business? Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurable interest prevents insurance from becoming gambling by ensuring the insured has a legal financial stake in the subject, meaning they suffer a real loss if it's damaged.

shortCQData.question: What is Proximate Cause?
shortCQData.answer: Proximate cause is the direct, immediate, and effective cause of a loss that is covered under the insurance policy.

shortCQData.question: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: Insurance is meant for protection and restoring the insured to their original position (Indemnity), whereas gambling is done to create a profit from chance.

shortCQData.question: What is the full form of IDRA?
shortCQData.answer: IDRA stands for Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority.

shortCQData.question: Insurance is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
shortCQData.answer: It means the insurer promises to compensate the insured only for the actual amount of loss suffered, ensuring no financial gain from the accident.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Robin lives with his father in his father's house. Thinking that the house might be damaged by an earthquake, Mr. Robin went to insure the house, but the insurance company refused to insure it. Later, Mr. Robin's father insured the house in his own name. After insuring, Mr. Robin's father started finishing the work on the second floor without informing the insurance company, causing the house to lean to one side. When Mr. Robin's father filed an insurance claim, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Mortality Table?
questions.answer: A Mortality Table is a statistical table that shows the probability of death for persons at each age, used by life insurance companies to calculate premiums.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by the Principle of Indemnity?
questions.answer: The Principle of Indemnity states that insurance is meant to restore the insured to their previous financial position after a loss, ensuring they do not make a profit from the disaster.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance company not agree to Mr. Robin's insurance proposal? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Robin tried to insure a house that he does not own (it belongs to his father).
Analysis: To insure any property, the person must have a legal financial interest in it, known as 'Insurable Interest'. Since Robin doesn't own the house, he suffers no direct financial loss if it is damaged.
Conclusion: The proposal was rejected due to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Robin's father's damaged house eligible for the insurance claim mentioned in the stem? Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: The father increased the risk (construction) without informing the insurer.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith', both parties must disclose all material facts. Any change in the nature of the risk must be reported.
Evaluation: By hiding the construction work, the contract was breached.
Decision: No, the father is not eligible for the claim as he failed to maintain transparency regarding the increased risk.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Bari is an established industrialist. To expand his industry, he wants to take on a project that requires a large investment and involves high risk. Fearing the loss of investable capital, he went to an insurance company to insure it, but the insurance company expressed inability to insure.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Double Insurance?
questions.answer: Double Insurance is a situation where the same subject matter or risk is insured with two or more insurance companies for the same period.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by Financial Risk?
questions.answer: Financial Risk refers to the possibility of monetary loss resulting from an event, where the impact can be measured and expressed in terms of money.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of risk is included in Mr. Bari's investable capital mentioned in the stem? Explain in light of insurance.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Bari is investing in a high-risk expansion project.
Analysis: In insurance, risks that arise from business decisions, market fluctuations, or investment strategies are called 'Speculative Risks'. These involve a chance of both gain and loss.
Conclusion: Mr. Bari's risk is a 'Speculative Risk'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Do you think it was reasonable for the insurance company to refuse Mr. Bari's insurance proposal? Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurer refused to cover an investment-based business risk.
Analysis: Insurance companies only cover 'Pure Risks' (events where there is only a chance of loss or no loss, like fire or theft). They do not cover business investment losses.
Evaluation: Speculative risks are generally uninsurable.
Decision: Yes, it was reasonable for the insurance company to refuse, as they cannot bear the burden of a businessman's commercial investment outcomes.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Jashim is a government employee. For convenience in commuting to his office, he purchased a motorcycle and insured it with an insurance company for its full value of 2 lakh BDT. When the bike was damaged in an accident, he received 1 lakh BDT in compensation from the insurance company according to the contract. Later, when the insurance company sold the damaged motorcycle for fifty thousand BDT, Mr. Jashim claimed a portion of that money.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurable Interest?
questions.answer: Insurable Interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a loss from its damage.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by the Principle of Utmost Good Faith?
questions.answer: The Principle of Utmost Good Faith implies that both the insurer and the insured must voluntarily disclose all relevant and material facts truthfully when entering an insurance contract.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which insurance principle did Mr. Jashim receive less compensation than the full value of the property? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The bike was insured for 2 lakh BDT, but he only received 1 lakh BDT.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Indemnity', the insurer pays only the 'actual loss' suffered, not the sum insured, to prevent the insured from profiting.
Conclusion: He received less due to the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logical validity of Mr. Jashim's claim for a portion of the money from the motorcycle sold by the insurance company.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurer paid the compensation and then sold the scrap.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', once the insurer pays the full loss, they gain legal ownership of whatever remains of the damaged property (salvage).
Evaluation: Mr. Jashim has already been compensated for his loss; he has no further right to the property.
Decision: Mr. Jashim's claim is illogical. According to the Principle of Subrogation, the proceeds from the sale of the scrap belong entirely to the insurance company.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Jamal is a bus driver. Considering his profession risky, he took a 10-year insurance policy for his family members. After paying premiums for 3 years, he died in an accident. When Mr. Jamal's wife raised an insurance claim, the insurance company paid the full claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Surrender Value?
questions.answer: Surrender Value is the amount an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they voluntarily terminate the policy before its maturity or before the insured event occurs.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by a Mortality Table?
questions.answer: A Mortality Table is a statistical chart showing the probability of death at different ages, which insurers use to determine life insurance premiums and risk levels.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Jamal take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Jamal took a 10-year policy for his family and the claim was paid upon his "death."
Analysis: A contract that pays a specific sum to the beneficiary upon the death of the insured or after a specific period is a Life Insurance policy. Since it had a 10-year term, it is an 'Endowment Life Insurance' policy.
Conclusion: Mr. Jamal took an 'Endowment Life Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance company paying the full claim without the full premium being paid.
questions.answer:
Data: Only 3 years of premiums were paid out of 10, yet the "full claim" was settled.
Analysis: Life insurance is a 'Contract of Certainty,' not just indemnity. Once the insured event (death) occurs, the insurer is legally bound to pay the sum assured, regardless of how many premiums were paid.
Evaluation: The remaining premiums are usually waived or adjusted, as the primary purpose of the contract was to provide financial security upon death.
Decision: The action is logical and legally valid under life insurance law, as the death of the insured triggers the full payment of the sum assured.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Pikul executed insurance contracts with two companies, 'Alpha' and 'Beta', for his factory. In an accident, his factory property worth 2 lakh BDT was damaged. According to the contract, both companies jointly paid Mr. Pikul a total compensation of 2 lakh BDT equally. Later, Mr. Pikul sold the damaged property for 50,000 BDT. Upon hearing the news, both insurance companies claimed that sale price.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Surrender Value?
questions.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount offered by an insurance company to the policyholder upon the cancellation of a life insurance policy before it reaches maturity.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Insurance is a system of risk distribution—Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is a system of risk distribution because it collects small premiums from a large number of people exposed to similar risks to form a fund, which is then used to compensate the few who actually suffer a loss.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle of insurance did the companies provide compensation to Mr. Pikul? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Two companies, Alpha and Beta, shared the 2 lakh BDT loss "equally."
Analysis: When the same risk is insured with multiple companies, and they share the loss proportionately so the insured doesn't profit, it is the 'Principle of Contribution'.
Conclusion: The companies paid him based on the 'Principle of Contribution'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logic of both companies' claim on the sale price of the property in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurer paid the full 2 lakh loss, and then Mr. Pikul sold the scrap (salvage) for 50,000 BDT.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once an insurer pays the full compensation, all legal rights to the damaged property pass to the insurer.
Evaluation: If Mr. Pikul keeps the 50,000, he would be making a profit (2 lakh + 50,000), which violates the Principle of Indemnity.
Decision: The claim by the companies is logical and legally correct. Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', the proceeds from the salvage belong to the insurers.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Abid Khondokar executed a 1-year insurance contract with 'Protasha Co. Ltd.' for his house. After the expiry of the term, Mr. Abid presented an insurance claim to 'Protasha Co. Ltd.' The company immediately refused to pay the insurance claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurable Interest?
questions.answer: Insurable Interest is the legal right to insure some property or life, where the person would suffer a financial loss if the property is damaged or the person dies.

questions.label: b
questions.text: How is risk-related hindrance removed? Explain.
questions.answer: Risk-related hindrance is removed through insurance, where the financial burden of a potential loss is transferred from an individual or business to an insurance company in exchange for a premium.

questions.label: c
questions.text: As which party to the insurance contract is Mr. Abid Khondokar regarded in the stem? Discuss.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Abid Khondokar is the one who "executed the contract" for "his house" and "presented the claim."
Analysis: In an insurance contract, the person whose risk is covered and who pays the premium is the 'Insured' or 'Policyholder'.
Conclusion: Mr. Abid is the 'Insured'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is it logical for Protasha Company to refuse the insurance claim? Analyze your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: The contract was for a "house" (General Insurance) for "1 year," and he claimed money after the "term expired" without mentioning a loss.
Analysis: Unlike life insurance, general insurance (Fire/Property) is a 'Contract of Indemnity'. If no loss occurs during the term, the insurer does not pay anything.
Evaluation: Since the house was not damaged, there is no loss to compensate.
Decision: Yes, the refusal is logical. In general insurance, the claim is only payable if a specific loss occurs within the policy period; otherwise, the premium is considered the cost of protection.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Hashem insured his private car by paying a certain amount of money. Recently, his car was damaged in an accident. After presenting an insurance claim, the insurer paid the claim as per the contract. Mr. Hashem claimed the money obtained from selling the wreckage of the car, but the insurer rejected his claim for that money.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome resulting from an action or an event, specifically the uncertainty regarding financial loss.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
questions.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they arise from natural disasters (like earthquakes or floods) or macro-economic shifts (like sudden inflation or political instability) that individuals or businesses cannot predict or prevent through internal measures.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle did Mr. Hashem receive the insurance claim from the insurer? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Hashem received compensation for the actual damage to his car.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Indemnity' ensures that the insured is compensated for the actual financial loss suffered, restoring them to their pre-loss position without allowing any profit.
Conclusion: He received the claim based on the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurer's rejection of Mr. Hashem's subsequent claim mentioned in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Hashem wanted the money from the "wreckage" (salvage) after already receiving the full claim.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once the insurer pays the full loss, the ownership rights of the damaged property pass to the insurer.
Evaluation: If he kept both the claim and the wreckage money, he would profit from the accident.
Decision: The rejection is logical. Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', the insurer is the legal owner of the wreckage once the claim is settled.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Kabir lives in a house rented from his friend Mr. Joynal. Fearing that the house might be damaged due to an earthquake, Mr. Kabir wanted to execute an insurance contract with an insurance company, but the insurer expressed inability. Later, Mr. Joynal executed an insurance contract for his house. After executing the contract, he tried to change the structure of the house without informing the insurance company, causing the house to lean partially to one side. When Mr. Joynal claimed compensation, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Speculative Risk?
questions.answer: Speculative risk is a type of risk that offers the possibility of either a gain or a loss, such as investing in the stock market or a new business venture.

questions.label: b
questions.text: How does insurance distribute risk? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance distributes risk by pooling the premiums of many individuals exposed to similar risks. This collective fund is then used to pay for the losses of the few who actually suffer an unfortunate event, effectively spreading the financial burden across the group.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance company refuse Mr. Kabir's proposal to insure the house? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Kabir tried to insure a "rented" house he does not own.
Analysis: To insure any property, one must have 'Insurable Interest,' meaning a legal financial stake where the person benefits from the property's safety and suffers from its damage.
Conclusion: The proposal was rejected due to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Joynal eligible to receive the insurance claim in light of the stem? Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Joynal "changed the structure" without "informing the insurance company."
Analysis: The 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' requires both parties to disclose all material facts and changes that affect the risk level. Structural changes increase risk significantly.
Evaluation: By not informing the insurer, Mr. Joynal breached the contract terms.
Decision: No, Mr. Joynal is not eligible for the claim because he violated the principle of transparency and altered the risk nature without consent.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Rahat insured his private car for 25 lakh BDT. The car was completely damaged in an accident. When Mr. Rahat presented the claim, the insurance company paid full compensation. Meanwhile, a businessman from Armani Tola, Dhaka, bought the damaged car for 50,000 BDT. Mr. Rahat claimed the money received from the sale of the car, but the insurance company refused to pay it.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the Principle of Indemnity?
questions.answer: The Principle of Indemnity is a rule in insurance stating that the insurer will pay only the actual amount of the loss suffered, ensuring the insured does not profit from the insurance.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because both the insurer and the insured are legally bound to disclose all material facts and information truthfully to ensure a fair assessment of risk.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Under which principle did Mr. Rahat receive money from the insurance company? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Rahat received "full compensation" for the damaged car.
Analysis: General insurance contracts are designed to cover the financial loss exactly as it occurred, which is the core of the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: He received the money under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Highlight the logic behind the insurance company not fulfilling Mr. Rahat's latest claim.
questions.answer:
Data: The damaged car (scrap) was sold for 50,000 BDT, which Rahat claimed after being fully compensated.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Subrogation' dictates that once a loss is fully settled, the remains of the property belong to the insurer.
Evaluation: Providing the sale proceeds to Mr. Rahat would result in him receiving 25.5 lakh total for a 25 lakh loss.
Decision: The refusal is logical and legally sound. According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', the insurer retains the proceeds from the salvage to offset the compensation they paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Khan executed insurance contracts with two companies, 'Popular' and 'Green', for a factory. In an accident, property worth 1 lakh BDT in Mr. Khan's factory was damaged. According to the contract, both companies jointly paid Mr. Khan a total compensation of 1 lakh BDT at an equal rate. Later, Mr. Khan sold the damaged property for 20,000 BDT. Upon hearing the news, both companies presented a claim on this sale price.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is insurance?
questions.answer: Insurance is a legal contract where one party (the insurer) agrees to compensate another party (the insured) for a specific loss or damage in exchange for a fee called a premium.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest means the legal right of the insured to insure an object or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle of the insurance contract did both companies provide compensation to Mr. Khan? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Two companies, Popular and Green, shared the 1 lakh BDT loss "at an equal rate."
Analysis: When the same risk is insured with multiple insurers, they share the loss proportionately so the insured doesn't profit. This is the 'Principle of Contribution'.
Conclusion: The companies paid him based on the 'Principle of Contribution'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logic of both companies' claim on the sale price of the damaged property.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurers paid the full 1 lakh loss, but Mr. Khan kept the 20,000 BDT from the scrap sale.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once the insurer pays the full compensation, the rights to the salvage (wreckage) pass to the insurer.
Evaluation: If Mr. Khan keeps the money, he would receive 1.2 lakh for a 1 lakh loss, violating the Principle of Indemnity.
Decision: The companies' claim is logical. Legally, the sale proceeds belong to the insurers to prevent the insured from profiting.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Karim is the owner of a ship named 'MV Surma'. He insured the ship for one crore BDT with 'XYZ' Insurance Company. For one-third of that risk, 'XYZ' Insurance Company decided to execute another insurance contract with 'PQR' Insurance Company.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is insurance?
questions.answer: Insurance is a contract of indemnity (except for life insurance) where the insurer promises to make good the loss of the insured in exchange for a premium.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance important for the expansion of international trade? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is vital for international trade because it covers high-risk maritime perils (like shipwrecks or piracy), providing financial security to exporters and importers, which encourages them to engage in large-scale global commerce.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Karim take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Karim insured his "ship" (MV Surma) for a specific amount.
Analysis: Insurance that covers ships, cargo, or freight against perils of the sea is known as Marine Insurance.
Conclusion: Mr. Karim took a 'Marine Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the subsequent decision taken by 'XYZ' Insurance Company in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: XYZ Company insured a portion (one-third) of their risk with another company, PQR.
Analysis: When an insurance company transfers a portion of its accepted risk to another insurance company to protect itself from a heavy loss, it is called 'Re-insurance'.
Evaluation: This allows XYZ to manage its liability and remain solvent in case of a massive claim.
Decision: The decision is highly logical. Re-insurance ensures financial stability for the primary insurer and helps them handle risks beyond their individual capacity.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Rifat insured his personal private car for 25 lakh BDT with 'Fuel Insurance Company'. The car was completely damaged in an accident on the Dhaka-Chittagong highway. Subsequently, when Mr. Rifat presented an insurance claim, the insurance company paid the full amount. Later, the insurance company sold the damaged car for 4 lakh BDT and kept the entire amount. However, when Mr. Rifat claimed the money obtained from selling the damaged car, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Fundamental Risk?
questions.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire economy or a large number of people simultaneously, such as war, inflation, or natural disasters like floods.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is the nearest cause of loss called? Explain.
questions.answer: The nearest cause of loss is called 'Causa Proxima' (Proximate Cause). It means that the insurer only pays for a loss if it was directly caused by a peril specifically covered in the insurance policy.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle did Mr. Rifat receive the insurance claim from the insurance institution? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Rifat received "full compensation" (25 lakh) for his car.
Analysis: The core principle of general insurance is to compensate the insured for the actual financial loss incurred, which is known as the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: He received the claim under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is it logical for the insurance company to pay the money obtained from selling the damaged car to Mr. Rifat? Give your opinion in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Rifat was fully compensated, but he still wants the 4 lakh BDT from the scrap sale.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a full claim is settled, the ownership of the damaged property (wreckage) shifts to the insurer.
Evaluation: Rifat has already been restored to his financial position; keeping the scrap money would mean a profit.
Decision: No, it is not logical to pay Rifat the sale proceeds. According to the Principle of Subrogation, that money legally belongs to the insurance company.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. 'P' lives in a three-story house registered in his father's name. Considering future accidents, Mr. 'P' went to an insurance company to insure the house, but they refused to insure it. Consequently, Mr. 'P's father insured the house himself. Later, Mr. 'P' started working on a fourth floor without informing the insurance company. This caused the house to lean to the left. When Mr. 'P's father filed an insurance claim, the company refused to fulfill it.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
questions.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full compensation for a loss, they acquire all legal rights and ownership of the damaged property (salvage) from the insured.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a joint system of risk distribution? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is a joint system of risk distribution because it collects small premiums from many people exposed to similar risks to create a common fund. This fund is then used to compensate the few who actually suffer a loss, effectively spreading the burden.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance company not agree to Mr. 'P's proposal in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. 'P' tried to insure a house registered in his "father's name."
Analysis: A person can only insure something if they have a legal financial stake in it, meaning they benefit from its safety and suffer from its loss. This is known as 'Insurable Interest'. Since P doesn't own the house, he has no insurable interest.
Conclusion: The proposal was rejected due to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance company's refusal to fulfill the insurance claim for Mr. 'P's father.
questions.answer:
Data: The father started construction on a "fourth floor" without "informing the company," which increased the risk.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith', the insured must disclose any material changes to the property. Structural changes significantly alter the risk profile.
Evaluation: By hiding the construction, the contract was breached.
Decision: The refusal is logical. The insurer is not liable for damages caused by an undeclared increase in risk that violates the terms of transparency.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Aslam is a businessman. He purchased a car for commuting and insured it for 25 lakh BDT. Sometime later, the car was completely damaged in an accident, and Mr. Aslam presented an insurance claim. Subsequently, the insurance company provided the claimed compensation. Meanwhile, the insurance company sold the damaged car to a workshop in Dhaka for 50,000 BDT. When Mr. Aslam claimed the money from the sale, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is an Insurance Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the specific amount of money paid periodically by the insured to the insurance company in exchange for the risk coverage provided by the insurance policy.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurable interest important in the insurance business? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurable interest is important because it prevents insurance from becoming a form of gambling. It ensures that the person taking the policy has a genuine financial relationship with the subject and will actually suffer a loss if damage occurs.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle did Mr. Aslam receive money from the insurance institution? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Aslam received compensation for the "actual damage" to his car.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Indemnity' ensures that the insured is returned to their financial position prior to the loss, preventing them from making a profit.
Conclusion: He received the claim based on the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: What is the logic behind the insurance company not fulfilling Mr. Aslam's latest claim? Analyze in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Aslam wants the "sale proceeds" of the scrap after being fully compensated.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once an insurer pays the full loss, the ownership of the salvage (wreckage) passes to the insurer.
Evaluation: If Aslam gets both, he would receive more than his actual loss.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Legally, the 50,000 BDT belongs to the insurer to offset the 25 lakh compensation they already paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Najib took an insurance policy for his friend Raju's property. While taking the policy, Mr. Najib stated that the property belonged to him. Later, the property was damaged by an insured event. In this situation, when Najib claimed compensation, the insurance company refused to fulfill it.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility or uncertainty of a financial loss or an unfavorable outcome resulting from a specific event.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What do you mean by Surrender Value?
questions.answer: Surrender value is the cash amount that an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate a life insurance policy before its maturity date.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Explain the reason for not paying the insurance claim to Mr. Najib.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Najib lied, stating the property "belonged to him" when it actually belonged to "his friend Raju."
Analysis: Every insurance contract requires 'Insurable Interest.' Najib has no legal ownership of Raju's property, and therefore suffers no direct financial loss.
Conclusion: The claim was refused because Najib lacked 'Insurable Interest' and provided false information.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Which principle of insurance will be violated if Mr. Najib is compensated? Evaluate.
questions.answer:
Data: Najib claimed the property was his (misrepresentation of ownership).
Analysis: This primarily violates the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' (disclosure of truth) and the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.
Evaluation: If paid, Najib would profit from the damage to someone else's property, which turns insurance into a wager.
Decision: Paying him would violate the 'Principle of Insurable Interest' and the 'Principle of Indemnity', as the insurance contract would no longer be a protective agreement but a fraudulent gain.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Usmi has a car. He executed an insurance contract with Jonaki Insurance Company, valuing the car at 30 lakh BDT. The car was damaged in an accident. Mr. Usmi claimed full compensation from the insurance company. Considering the logic of the claim, the insurance company paid full compensation. When the insurance company claimed the damaged car, Mr. Usmi refused to hand it over but was later forced to do so.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the fixed amount of money that the insured pays to the insurance company periodically in exchange for the risk coverage provided.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
questions.answer: Insurance is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because both the insurer and the insured are legally bound to disclose all relevant and material facts truthfully to ensure a fair assessment of the risk.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Under which principle did Mr. Usmi receive compensation? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Usmi received 30 lakh BDT for the actual damage caused to his car.
Analysis: The core principle of general insurance is to compensate the insured only for the actual loss suffered, restoring them to their original financial position.
Conclusion: He received the money under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Was the refusal to hand over the damaged car in the stem logical? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Usmi was fully compensated but wanted to keep the "damaged car" (salvage).
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once the insurer pays the full loss, they gain the legal ownership of whatever remains of the property.
Evaluation: If Usmi kept both, he would profit from the accident, which violates insurance laws.
Decision: No, the refusal was not logical. Legally, the damaged car belongs to the insurer under the Principle of Subrogation.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Miraj executed a 6-month insurance contract with ABC Insurance Company for his residential building. After the term expired, Mr. Miraj filed an insurance claim with the company. ABC Company refused to pay Mr. Miraj's insurance claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome, specifically the uncertainty regarding a potential financial loss.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right to insure an object or life because the insured has a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged or destroyed.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance contract did Mr. Miraj execute in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Miraj insured a "residential building" for a short duration of "6 months."
Analysis: Insurance that covers physical property like buildings against risks like fire or natural disasters is categorized as Fire or General Insurance.
Conclusion: Mr. Miraj executed a 'Fire Insurance' or 'Property Insurance' contract.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is there any logic behind ABC Company's refusal to pay the insurance claim in light of insurance principles? Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: The term expired, but no damage was mentioned; Miraj still asked for a claim.
Analysis: General insurance is a 'Contract of Indemnity,' meaning the insurer only pays if a loss occurs within the policy period.
Evaluation: Unlike life insurance, there is no "maturity value" in property insurance if no accident happens.
Decision: Yes, the refusal is logical. Since no loss occurred during the 6-month term, the insurer is not obligated to pay any money.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Sinha took a 12 lakh BDT fire insurance policy for his goods warehouse. Considering the high risk, he executed another fire insurance policy of equal value for the same property with another company. Due to a fire, property worth 3 lakh BDT was damaged, and both insurance companies paid Mr. Sinha compensation at an equal rate. When the insurance companies claimed the remains of the damaged property, Mr. Sinha refused to provide it.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is uncertainty?
questions.answer: Uncertainty is a state where the outcome of an event is unknown and cannot be accurately predicted or measured.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
questions.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because it relies on the voluntary and honest disclosure of all material facts by both the insurer and the insured to maintain the validity of the contract.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which principle of insurance was followed in providing compensation in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Two companies shared the 3 lakh BDT loss "at an equal rate" for the same property.
Analysis: When a person takes policies from multiple companies for the same risk, they share the loss proportionately so the insured doesn't profit. This is the 'Principle of Contribution'.
Conclusion: The 'Principle of Contribution' was followed.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance companies' claim in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurers paid for the 3 lakh loss and then claimed the "remains" (scrap).
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', the insurer steps into the shoes of the insured regarding the damaged property once the claim is settled.
Evaluation: Mr. Sinha has already been compensated; keeping the remains would result in a gain beyond his loss.
Decision: The claim by the companies is logical and legally sound. Under the Principle of Subrogation, the companies own the salvage in proportion to the compensation they paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Akiz started a shop. On a friend's advice, he executed a 1-year fire insurance contract worth 2 lakh BDT with Padma Company. For this, Mr. Akiz paid Padma Company a lump sum of 20,000 BDT. Later, when fire damage occurred, the insurance company provided compensation.

questions.label: a
questions.text: Who is the Insurer?
questions.answer: The insurer is the party or company that agrees to provide compensation for a specific loss or damage in exchange for a premium.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of a person to insure a subject matter because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a direct monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What is the money paid to Padma Company in the stem called? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Akiz paid a "lump sum of 20,000 BDT" for the insurance coverage.
Analysis: The consideration or fee paid by the insured to the insurance company in exchange for the risk coverage is called the 'Premium'.
Conclusion: The money paid is called the 'Insurance Premium'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Insurance is a financial security measure against potential loss—give your opinion in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Akiz took a policy and when a fire occurred, the company "provided compensation."
Analysis: Without insurance, Akiz would have to bear the entire 2 lakh loss alone, which could bankrupt his shop.
Evaluation: By paying a small premium (20k), he transferred the large potential risk to the company.
Decision: I agree with the statement. The stem proves that insurance acted as a safety net, ensuring that Mr. Akiz's business survived despite the fire disaster.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Usmi has a car. He executed an insurance contract with Jonaki Insurance Company, valuing the car at 30 lakh BDT. The car was damaged in an accident. Mr. Usmi claimed full compensation from the insurance company. Considering the logic of the claim, the insurance company paid full compensation. When the insurance company claimed the damaged car, Mr. Usmi refused to hand it over but was later forced to do so.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
