questions.answer: An insurance premium is the amount paid periodically or in a lump sum by the policyholder to the insurer for covering a specific risk.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith?
questions.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because both parties must voluntarily and honestly disclose all material facts related to the risk to ensure the contract is fair and valid.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Under which principle did Mr. Usmi receive compensation? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Usmi was paid the "full compensation" based on the car's value and actual loss.
Analysis: The principle that aims to return the insured to their exact financial position prior to the loss is the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: He received compensation under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Was the refusal to hand over the damaged car in the stem logical? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Usmi received full payment but tried to keep the "damaged car" (salvage).
Analysis: The 'Principle of Subrogation' states that after paying a full claim, the remains of the property belong to the insurer.
Evaluation: If he kept both, he would profit from the disaster.
Decision: No, the refusal was not logical. According to the Principle of Subrogation, the insurer is the rightful owner of the wreckage once the claim is settled.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shahin rented his warehouse to Shapla Traders. Shapla Traders kept business goods in the warehouse and executed an insurance contract against the risk of fire. It is known that when Mr. Shahin tried to execute an insurance contract for the goods kept in the warehouse, the company refused. One day a fire broke out and the goods were burnt to ashes, and the insurance company paid full compensation. Later, it was found that there were some partially good items under the debris, which a businessman agreed to buy for 50,000 BDT.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurance?
questions.answer: Insurance is a legal agreement where an insurer promises to compensate the insured for a specific loss in exchange for a premium.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a system of risk distribution?
questions.answer: It is called a system of risk distribution because it pools the small premiums of many people to pay for the large losses of a few, effectively spreading the financial burden across the group.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which feature of insurance was missing due to which the company refused to contract with Mr. Shahin? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Shahin (the owner of the building) tried to insure the "goods" inside, but the company refused.
Analysis: A person can only insure something if they suffer a financial loss from its damage. Shahin doesn't own the goods; Shapla Traders does.
Conclusion: The company refused due to a lack of 'Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Who will receive the money from selling the partially burnt goods? Comment in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurer paid the "full compensation" for the goods.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a loss is fully compensated, all rights to the remaining materials pass to the insurance company.
Evaluation: Shapla Traders cannot keep the 50,000 BDT as they have already been fully paid for the loss.
Decision: The insurance company will receive the money. This ensures the insured does not make a profit, maintaining the Principle of Indemnity.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shahrukh lives in the same house with his father. The house is in his father's name. Thinking that the house might be damaged in an earthquake, Mr. Shahrukh went to insure it, but the insurance company refused. Then his father insured the house himself. Afterward, Mr. Shahrukh completed construction of the 2nd and 3rd floors without informing the company. Soon after, the house collapsed. When the father filed a claim, the company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable outcome or financial loss arising from an event whose occurrence is uncertain.

questions.label: b
questions.text: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is not gambling because its purpose is to protect against a potential loss and restore the insured to their previous position (Indemnity), whereas gambling is designed to create a profit or gain based purely on chance.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance company not agree to Mr. Shahrukh's proposal? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Shahrukh tried to insure a house that belongs to his "father," not himself.
Analysis: A legal requirement for insurance is 'Insurable Interest,' meaning the person must suffer a direct financial loss if the property is damaged. Since Shahrukh doesn't own it, he lacks this interest.
Conclusion: The proposal was rejected due to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Shahrukh's father eligible to receive the insurance claim in light of the stem? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: The father completed construction of two additional floors without "informing the company," which led to the collapse.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' requires the insured to disclose all material facts and changes that increase risk. Structural additions significantly change the risk profile.
Evaluation: By hiding the construction, the father breached the transparency of the contract.
Decision: No, he is not eligible. The company is justified in refusing the claim because the risk nature was altered without their consent, violating the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith'.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Kamal has been suffering from an incurable disease for a long time. He hid this information and took a life insurance policy for the financial security of his family. After paying a few installments, Mr. Kamal died, and his family filed a claim. The insurance company refused the claim and cancelled the contract.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the periodic payment made by the insured to the insurer in exchange for taking on the specified risk.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by risk?
questions.answer: Risk refers to the uncertainty regarding a loss; it is the measurable chance that an actual outcome will differ from an expected outcome.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which party to the insurance contract is Mr. Kamal? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Kamal is the person who "took the policy" and paid the installments.
Analysis: In an insurance contract, the person whose life or property is covered and who pays the premium is known as the 'Insured' or 'Policyholder'.
Conclusion: Mr. Kamal is the 'Insured'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logic behind the cancellation of the contract by the insurance company.
questions.answer:
Data: Kamal "hid the information" about his "incurable disease" while taking the policy.
Analysis: Insurance is a 'Contract of Utmost Good Faith' (Uberrimae Fidei). Both parties must disclose all material facts—in this case, health status is a critical factor for life insurance.
Evaluation: Hiding a terminal illness is a fundamental breach of trust and contract law.
Decision: The cancellation is highly logical. If the company had known about the disease, they might have rejected the policy or charged a much higher premium. Due to the fraud, the contract becomes void.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Mamun took an insurance policy for 10 years. According to the contract, if he dies within the specified time, his family will receive the claim, or he will receive it himself at the end of the term. When he filed a claim at the end of the term, the company found out that although his age was 45, he had mentioned it as 40 in the policy.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Re-insurance?
questions.answer: Re-insurance is a process where an insurance company transfers a portion of its own risk to another insurance company to protect itself from large losses.

questions.label: b
questions.text: How is Double Insurance done? Explain.
questions.answer: Double Insurance is done when a person insures the same subject matter (property or life) with two or more different insurance companies for the same period and risk.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Mamun take? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The policy pays if Mamun "dies within the term" OR "at the end of the term" to himself.
Analysis: A life insurance policy that pays the sum assured either upon death or after a fixed period (whichever comes first) is called an 'Endowment Policy'.
Conclusion: Mr. Mamun took an 'Endowment Life Insurance Policy'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Mamun eligible to receive the insurance claim? Analyze in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: Mamun lied about his age (stated 40 instead of 45).
Analysis: Age is a "material fact" in life insurance as it determines the risk and premium rate. Providing false information violates the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith'.
Evaluation: While life policies often have an "incontestability clause" after 2 years, fraud regarding age usually leads to an adjustment of the claim or cancellation.
Decision: He may not be eligible for the *full* claim. The company can either cancel the contract due to misrepresentation or, more commonly, deduct the difference in premiums he should have paid for age 45 from the final payout.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Akash is the owner of a garment industry. He executed an insurance contract with an insurance company for his garment industry and its valuable machinery. As the proper value of the garment industry and machinery was not mentioned in the insurance policy, the insurance company cancelled the executed insurance contract.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Fundamental Risk?
questions.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire society or a large number of people at once, such as war, inflation, or natural disasters.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Insurance is not a wager or gambling—Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is not gambling because it is meant to provide financial protection against an existing risk and restore the insured to their previous position (Indemnity), whereas gambling creates a new risk for the purpose of making a profit.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Akash take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Akash insured his "garment industry" and "valuable machinery."
Analysis: Insurance that covers physical assets, factories, and equipment against various risks is categorized as Property or Fire Insurance.
Conclusion: Mr. Akash took a 'Property/Fire Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze which principle's inconsistency led the insurance company to cancel the insurance contract.
questions.answer:
Data: The "proper value" was not mentioned in the policy.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' (Uberrimae Fidei) requires both parties to disclose all material facts accurately, including the correct value of the property.
Evaluation: Providing an incorrect or vague value prevents the insurer from calculating the correct premium and assessing the risk fairly.
Decision: The company cancelled the contract due to a violation of the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith'. Accurate valuation is a material fact, and its absence makes the contract voidable.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Nabil is an importer. He imports wheat from Russia by sea. To tackle maritime risks, he entered into an insurance contract with Sunsea Insurance Company with a condition of compensation up to 2 crore BDT in exchange for a premium of 25,000 BDT. While the ship was arriving at the port, it suddenly hit submerged ice and the ship and about 10 lakh BDT worth of wheat were damaged.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the consideration or fee paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the promise to cover a specific risk.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because it relies on the honest disclosure of all material facts by both parties; if any truth is hidden, the contract becomes legally invalid.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Nabil take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Nabil insured goods ("wheat") being transported "by sea" from Russia.
Analysis: Insurance that covers risks related to ships, cargo, and maritime transport is known as Marine Insurance.
Conclusion: Mr. Nabil took a 'Marine Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Will Mr. Nabil receive full compensation from the insurance company? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: The damage (10 lakh BDT) is within the policy limit (2 crore BDT).
Analysis: Marine insurance is a contract of indemnity. The insurer is liable to pay the 'actual loss' suffered by the insured due to an insured peril (hitting ice).
Evaluation: Since 10 lakh is less than the 2 crore limit and the cause (collision) is a maritime peril, Nabil is entitled to the loss amount.
Decision: Yes, Mr. Nabil will receive the 10 lakh BDT compensation, provided he followed all terms of the contract and disclosed all material facts.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Taher insured his institution for 50 lakh BDT. The institution was completely damaged in an accident, and Mr. Taher presented an insurance claim. The insurance company provided full compensation. When the insurance company claimed the recovered property, Mr. Taher initially refused to hand it over but was later forced to do so.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurable Interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure an object or life because they would suffer a financial loss if that object or life were harmed.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of utmost good faith because the insurer cannot physically inspect every minor detail of the risk and must rely on the truthful representation of facts provided by the insured.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Under which principle mentioned in the stem did Mr. Taher receive money from the insurance institution? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Taher was paid the "full compensation" for the total damage to his property.
Analysis: The core principle of general insurance is to compensate for the actual loss, which is the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: He received the claim under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Was Mr. Taher's decision to refuse handing over the property correct even after receiving full compensation? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: Taher received the full 50 lakh claim but tried to keep the "recovered property" (salvage).
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a loss is fully paid, the ownership of the remaining property passes to the insurer.
Evaluation: Keeping both the money and the salvage would result in Taher profiting from the accident.
Decision: No, his decision was incorrect. Under the Principle of Subrogation, the recovered property legally belongs to the insurance company once the full claim is settled.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Babul lives with his father in the same house. The house is in Babul's father's name. Thinking that the house might be damaged by an earthquake, Mr. Babul wanted to insure the house. When he went to the insurance institution, they refused. Then Mr. Babul's father insured the house himself. Afterward, Mr. Babul started working on the second floor without informing the insurance institution. Right after the work was finished, the house leaned to one side. When the father filed a claim, the company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is an Insurance Contract?
questions.answer: An insurance contract is a legal agreement where the insurer, in exchange for a premium, promises to compensate the insured for losses caused by specified risks or events.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of indemnity? Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because it aims to compensate the insured only for the actual loss suffered, ensuring they are returned to their previous financial position without making any profit from the disaster.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance institution not agree to Mr. Babul's proposal? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Babul tried to insure a house that is in his "father's name."
Analysis: A fundamental requirement for insurance is 'Insurable Interest,' meaning the person must have a legal financial stake in the property. Since Babul doesn't own the house, he wouldn't suffer a direct legal financial loss if it were damaged.
Conclusion: The proposal was rejected due to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Babul's father eligible to receive the insurance claim in light of the stem? Analyze.
questions.answer:
Data: The house leaned due to "second floor construction" which was done "without informing" the insurer.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith,' all material changes to the risk must be disclosed. Structural changes increase the risk profile.
Evaluation: By hiding the construction, the father breached the terms of the contract.
Decision: No, the father is not eligible. The insurer is justified in refusing the claim as the nature of the risk was altered without their knowledge or consent.

fullCQData.stem: Shahrukh lives in the same house with his father. The house is in Shahrukh's father's name. Thinking that the house might be damaged in an earthquake, Mr. Shahrukh went to insure the house, but the company refused. Then the father insured the house himself. Afterward, Shahrukh completed construction of the 2nd and 3rd floors without informing the company. Right after finishing, the house collapsed. When the father filed a claim, the company refused.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility or uncertainty of a financial loss or an unfavorable outcome resulting from an unexpected event.

questions.label: b
questions.text: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is not gambling because it is meant to provide protection against an existing potential loss (Indemnity), whereas gambling creates a new risk for the sole purpose of making a profit based on chance.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to which principle did the insurance company not agree to Mr. Shahrukh's proposal? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Shahrukh tried to insure property owned by his father.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Insurable Interest,' a person can only take out insurance if they have a legal relationship with the subject matter such that its damage would cause them a financial loss.
Conclusion: The company refused because Shahrukh lacked 'Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Is Mr. Shahrukh's father eligible to receive the insurance claim in light of the stem? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: Additional floors were built without "informing the company," leading to the collapse.
Analysis: Insurance is a 'Contract of Utmost Good Faith' (Uberrimae Fidei). The insured must report any changes that increase the risk.
Evaluation: Structural additions are material facts that affect the safety of the building and the insurer's liability.
Decision: In my opinion, the father is not eligible. The refusal is logical as the contract was breached when material facts regarding the increased risk were withheld.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Farid's only child is Joli. Joli is married. Thinking about the future of his married daughter, Mr. Farid wanted to open a life insurance policy in Joli's name, but the company refused. On the other hand, Joli's husband Roni opened a life insurance policy in Joli's name. However, while opening the policy, Roni did not mention Joli's illness to the insurance company.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: A premium is the consideration or fee paid by the insured to the insurance company in exchange for the financial protection provided by the policy.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of a person to insure someone or something because they benefit from its existence and would suffer a financial loss upon its death or destruction.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which principle of insurance did Roni violate while opening the life insurance policy in Joli's name? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Roni "did not mention Joli's illness" to the insurance company.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' requires the insured to disclose all material facts—especially health status in life insurance—truthfully.
Conclusion: Roni violated the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance company's refusal to let Mr. Farid open a life insurance policy for Joli in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Farid (father) tried to insure his "married daughter," but was refused.
Analysis: In many insurance jurisdictions, once a daughter is married and financially independent or dependent on her husband, the father's legal 'Insurable Interest' in her life may be considered limited or non-existent for new policies.
Evaluation: Insurance requires a direct financial relationship; if Joli is no longer a financial dependent of Farid, he lacks the necessary legal interest.
Decision: The refusal is logical based on the 'Principle of Insurable Interest,' as the father may not suffer a direct financial loss from the event, whereas a husband (Roni) clearly has that interest.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Azam and his wife executed an insurance contract with a low premium. Some time later, when Mr. Azam died, his wife filed a claim. The insurance company refused to pay and informed her that the insurance money would be received by their children after her death.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the amount of money paid by the insured to the insurance company periodically or in a lump sum in exchange for risk coverage.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Is an insurance contract possible without insurable interest? Explain.
questions.answer: No, an insurance contract is not legally valid without insurable interest. It prevents insurance from becoming gambling and ensures that the insured has a genuine financial stake in the subject matter.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Azam take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The policy was taken by a couple (husband and wife) and the benefit is paid only after both die.
Analysis: A life insurance policy that covers two lives and pays the sum assured only after the death of the last surviving person is called a 'Joint Life Annuity' or 'Joint Life Policy' with specific conditions. Since the money is promised to children after the wife's death, it functions as a 'Joint Life Survivorship' policy.
Conclusion: Mr. Azam took a 'Joint Life Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance company's refusal to fulfill the claim of Mr. Azam's wife in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The company said money will be paid to children only after the wife's death.
Analysis: In certain joint life policies, the claim is not triggered by the first death but by the last survivor's death. This is often done for legacy planning for children.
Evaluation: The terms of the specific policy clearly state that the payout happens after the second death.
Decision: The refusal is logical and legally valid. The insurance company is following the specific terms of the 'Joint Life Policy' agreed upon during the contract.

fullCQData.stem: A factory owner, thinking of potential losses, insured his factory for 5 lakh BDT. The factory was damaged in an accident, and the owner filed a claim. The insurance company provided full compensation. When the insurance company claimed the partially damaged property from the accident, the owner initially refused to hand it over but later handed it over to the company.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
questions.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full claim for a loss, they acquire all legal rights and ownership of the remaining damaged property (salvage).

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: The handover of property mentioned in the stem is related to which principle of insurance? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The owner had to "hand over" the damaged remains after receiving the "full claim."
Analysis: The principle that transfers the ownership of wreckage to the insurer after a total loss settlement is called the 'Principle of Subrogation'.
Conclusion: It is related to the 'Principle of Subrogation'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Was the owner's decision to refuse handing over the property correct even after receiving full compensation? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: The owner received the full 5 lakh compensation but wanted to keep the scrap/remains.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Indemnity,' insurance is only to cover the loss, not to make a profit. If he keeps the scrap and the money, he makes a profit.
Evaluation: The Principle of Subrogation legally prevents such profit-making.
Decision: No, the owner's decision was incorrect. Legally and ethically, after accepting full compensation, the remains of the property belong to the insurance company.

fullCQData.stem: Bexi Textile Mills Ltd. is a garment manufacturing company. It has 5 production machines of its own and uses 3 rented machines from another factory to meet extra demand. When the company wanted to insure all 8 machines, the insurance company only agreed to insure the 5 owned machines. Later, one insured machine was completely destroyed in a fire. The insurer paid full compensation as per the contract. After the accident, some parts of the destroyed machine were sold for 45,000 BDT. When Bexi Textile Mills Ltd. claimed that money, the insurer refused.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Ideal Moral Risk?
questions.answer: Ideal Moral Risk refers to a situation where the insured person is exceptionally honest, disciplined, and takes more care than a regular person to prevent any loss or damage to the insured property.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is an insurance contract called a contract of utmost good faith? Explain.
questions.answer: An insurance contract is called a contract of utmost good faith (Uberrimae Fidei) because it relies on the voluntary and honest disclosure of all material facts by both parties; if any truth is hidden, the contract becomes legally void.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to the lack of which element of the insurance contract did the insurer not insure the rented machines? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The company tried to insure 3 "rented" machines along with their own.
Analysis: A fundamental requirement for insurance is 'Insurable Interest,' meaning the person must have a legal financial stake in the property where they benefit from its safety and suffer from its loss. Since the 3 machines are rented, the legal ownership (and primary financial risk of loss) stays with the original owner.
Conclusion: The insurer refused due to the lack of 'Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurer's rejection of the final claim in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Bexi Textile Mills claimed the 45,000 BDT from the "sold parts" after receiving full compensation.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation,' once an insurer pays the full loss, they acquire all legal rights and ownership of the damaged remains (salvage).
Evaluation: If the company kept both the full claim and the sale money, they would profit from the fire.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Legally, the proceeds from the wreckage belong to the insurer to maintain the Principle of Indemnity.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Suman executed an insurance contract with two companies, 'A' and 'B', for his factory. In an accident, factory property worth 6 lakh BDT was damaged. According to the insurance contract, both companies provided compensation at an equal rate. When the insurance companies claimed a portion of the damaged property, Mr. Suman refused to give it.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Fundamental Risk?
questions.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that affects the entire economy or a large group of people simultaneously, such as war, massive floods, or sudden inflation.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is the nearest cause of loss called? Explain.
questions.answer: The nearest cause of loss is called 'Causa Proxima' (Proximate Cause). It means that for a claim to be valid, the loss must be directly caused by a peril that is explicitly covered in the insurance policy.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which principle of insurance was followed in providing compensation in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Two companies, A and B, shared the 6 lakh BDT loss "at an equal rate."
Analysis: When a property is insured with multiple insurers for the same risk, they contribute proportionately to the loss so the insured does not receive more than the actual loss.
Conclusion: This follows the 'Principle of Contribution'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance companies' claim in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurers paid for the loss and then claimed the "damaged property" (scrap).
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Subrogation,' the insurer steps into the legal shoes of the insured regarding the salvage once the full claim is settled.
Evaluation: Mr. Suman has already been restored to his previous financial position; keeping the scrap would lead to profit.
Decision: The companies' claim is logical and legally sound. According to the Principle of Subrogation, the remains of the property belong to the insurers in proportion to the compensation they paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shuvo insured his newly purchased car worth 25 lakh BDT with an insurance company. Sometime later, the car was completely destroyed in a sudden accident. The insurance company paid the full amount of 25 lakh BDT to Mr. Shuvo as an insurance claim and received 2 lakh BDT by selling the remains of the damaged car. When Mr. Shuvo claimed the 2 lakh BDT from the sale of the car, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Surrender Value?
questions.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount that a life insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate the policy before its maturity or before the insured event occurs.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
questions.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they originate from natural forces (earthquakes, cyclones) or systemic economic factors (market crashes) that an individual cannot prevent through their own actions or precautions.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which principle of insurance did the company follow to provide full compensation to Mr. Shuvo? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Shuvo received the "full 25 lakh BDT" for the actual loss of his car.
Analysis: General insurance is meant to restore the insured to their original financial position without allowing any profit, which is the core of the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: The company followed the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance company not paying the sale money of the damaged car to Mr. Shuvo.
questions.answer:
Data: Shuvo received full compensation and then asked for the 2 lakh BDT "sale money" of the wreckage.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Subrogation' dictates that after a full settlement, the insurer owns the salvage.
Evaluation: If Shuvo received 25 lakh + 2 lakh, he would end up with 27 lakh for a 25 lakh loss, which violates the indemnity rule.
Decision: The refusal is logical. According to the Principle of Subrogation, the 2 lakh BDT rightfully belongs to the insurance company.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Kaysar is the owner of a shipping company. He insures his ships. Recently, one of his ships sank after being caught in a cyclone. When he filed a claim, the insurer paid the full amount as compensation. Later, the ship was recovered, and the insurer sold it as scrap to a businessman. Knowing this, Mr. Kaysar claimed the sale proceeds, but the insurance institution rejected his claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the Principle of Subrogation?
questions.answer: The Principle of Subrogation states that once the insurer pays the full compensation for a loss to the insured, all legal rights and ownership of the remaining damaged property (salvage) pass to the insurer.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is there no insurance without a contract?
questions.answer: Insurance is a legal relationship between two parties where risks are transferred for a consideration (premium). Since it involves legal rights, obligations, and enforceability in court, it must be based on a formal contract.

questions.label: c
questions.text: In light of which principle did Mr. Kaysar receive the insurance claim from the insurer? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Kaysar received "full compensation" for his sunken ship.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Indemnity' states that the insurer will restore the insured to their original financial position after a loss, without allowing any profit.
Conclusion: He received the claim based on the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance company's rejection of Mr. Kaysar's subsequent money claim.
questions.answer:
Data: Kaysar was fully compensated but then claimed the "sale proceeds" of the recovered ship.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation,' the insurer gains ownership of the salvage after paying the full claim.
Evaluation: If Kaysar kept both, he would profit from the accident, which violates the indemnity rule.
Decision: The rejection is logical and legally sound. The insurer is the rightful owner of the recovered ship's scrap value to offset the compensation paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ananta Jalil owns an export-oriented garment factory. He insured it with an insurance company for 1,00,00,000 BDT. The factory was completely destroyed in an accident, and Mr. Ananta Jalil presented his claim. The insurer paid the entire claim amount. Initially, he refused to hand over the recovered items to the insurance company but was eventually forced to do so.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurable Interest?
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to take out insurance because they have a financial relationship with the subject matter and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: b
questions.text: For which insurance is the principle of subrogation not applicable? Explain.
questions.answer: The Principle of Subrogation is not applicable to Life Insurance. This is because human life is invaluable and cannot be measured in money, so life insurance is a 'Contract of Certainty,' not a 'Contract of Indemnity.'

questions.label: c
questions.text: The handover of property is consistent with which principle of insurance? Explain in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Ananta Jalil had to "hand over the recovered items" after receiving the "entire claim amount."
Analysis: The principle that transfers ownership of wreckage/remains to the insurer after a total loss settlement is called the 'Principle of Subrogation'.
Conclusion: It is consistent with the 'Principle of Subrogation'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Mr. Ananta Jalil has no right to claim the recovered items—Do you agree with this statement? Comment.
questions.answer:
Data: Ananta Jalil was fully compensated (1 crore BDT) for his destroyed factory.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Indemnity' and 'Subrogation,' the insured cannot claim both the compensation and the salvage value.
Evaluation: Once the insurer pays for the "total loss," the remains are no longer the property of the insured.
Decision: Yes, I agree. Legally, once the full claim is accepted, the rights to the salvage belong solely to the insurance company to prevent the insured from making a profit.

fullCQData.stem: Monir learns from his friend about a type of business that provides financial assistance against various risks related to human life and property. Currently, this business is becoming popular among everyone. Moreover, the business is regulated by a powerful institution.
fullCQData.meta: Jalalabad Cantt Public School & College, Sylhet · 2026

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Double Insurance?
questions.answer: Double Insurance occurs when a person insures the same subject matter or risk with two or more different insurance companies for the same period.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What do you mean by the Principle of Contribution? Explain.
questions.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a person has multiple insurance policies for the same risk, all insurers will share the loss proportionately so the insured only receives the actual loss amount.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which business is mentioned in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The business provides "financial assistance against risks related to life and property."
Analysis: A system that provides financial protection against uncertainty in exchange for a premium is called Insurance.
Conclusion: The business mentioned is the 'Insurance Business'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the contribution of the regulatory institution in the development of the business mentioned in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The business is "regulated by a powerful institution."
Analysis: In Bangladesh, this institution is the 'Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority' (IDRA).
Evaluation: IDRA protects policyholders' interests, ensures transparency, and monitors companies to prevent fraud.
Decision: IDRA's contribution is vital; it builds public trust, stabilizes the market, and ensures that insurance companies fulfill their claims, leading to the overall growth of the industry.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shahin rented his warehouse to Shapla Traders. Shapla Traders kept business goods in the warehouse and executed an insurance contract against the risk of fire. It is known that when Mr. Shahin tried to execute an insurance contract for the goods kept in the warehouse, the company refused. One day a fire broke out and the goods were burnt to ashes, and the insurance company paid full compensation. Later, it was found that there were some partially good items under the debris, which a businessman agreed to buy for 50,000 BDT.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurance?
questions.answer: Insurance is a legal agreement where an insurer, in exchange for a premium, promises to compensate the insured for a specific loss or damage.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurance called a system of risk distribution?
questions.answer: Insurance is called a risk distribution system because it collects small premiums from many people exposed to similar risks to create a fund, which is then used to pay for the losses of the few who actually suffer.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which feature of insurance was missing due to which the insurance company refused to contract with Mr. Shahin? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Shahin (the building owner) tried to insure the "goods" inside, but the company refused.
Analysis: A person can only insure something if they suffer a financial loss from its damage. Shahin doesn't own the goods; Shapla Traders does.
Conclusion: The company refused due to a lack of 'Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Who will receive the money from selling the partially burnt goods? Comment in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: The insurer paid the "full compensation" for the goods.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a loss is fully compensated, all rights to the remaining materials pass to the insurance company.
Evaluation: Shapla Traders cannot keep the 50,000 BDT as they have already been fully paid for the loss.
Decision: The insurance company will receive the money. This ensures the insured does not make a profit, maintaining the Principle of Indemnity.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ajmal owns a steel factory. Due to the risky nature of the work, he wants to insure the factory with two insurance companies. For this purpose, he executes two separate insurance contracts of 1 crore BDT each with 'Alpha Insurance Company' and 'Beta Insurance Company'. Later, due to an accident, the factory suffers a loss of 30 lakh BDT, and he files separate claims of 30 lakh BDT with each company. 'Alpha' and 'Beta' refuse to pay 30 lakh each and instead pay him 15 lakh each.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is risk?
questions.answer: Risk is the possibility of an unfavorable or unexpected outcome resulting from an action or an event, specifically the uncertainty regarding financial loss.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by insurable interest? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right of the insured to insure a property or life because they have a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance did Mr. Ajmal take for his factory? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Ajmal insured the same "factory" with two different companies (Alpha and Beta).
Analysis: When the same subject matter is insured with more than one insurer for the same risk and period, it is called 'Double Insurance'.
Conclusion: Mr. Ajmal took 'Double Insurance'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the two insurance companies' refusal to pay Mr. Ajmal's separate 30 lakh BDT claims.
questions.answer:
Data: Total loss was 30 lakh, but Ajmal claimed 30 lakh from *each* (total 60 lakh).
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Contribution' and 'Indemnity', the insured cannot profit from insurance. Multiple insurers must share the actual loss proportionately.
Evaluation: Paying 60 lakh for a 30 lakh loss would violate the indemnity principle.
Decision: The refusal is logical. The companies are only liable to pay 15 lakh each so that the total compensation equals the actual loss of 30 lakh BDT.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shuvo insured his newly purchased car worth 25 lakh BDT with an insurance company. Sometime later, the car was completely destroyed in a sudden accident. The insurance company paid the full amount of 25 lakh BDT to Mr. Shuvo as an insurance claim and received 2 lakh BDT by selling the remains of the damaged car. When Mr. Shuvo claimed the 2 lakh BDT from the sale of the car, the insurance company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Surrender Value?
questions.answer: Surrender Value is the cash amount that an insurance company pays to a policyholder if they decide to terminate a life insurance policy before its maturity.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Some risks are uncontrollable—Explain.
questions.answer: Some risks are uncontrollable because they arise from natural forces (earthquakes, cyclones) or macro-economic shifts that individuals cannot prevent through their own actions.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which principle of insurance did the company follow to provide full compensation to Mr. Shuvo? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Shuvo was paid the "full 25 lakh BDT" for the actual loss of his car.
Analysis: The principle that aims to return the insured to their exact financial position prior to the loss is the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: The company followed the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance company not paying the sale money of the damaged car to Mr. Shuvo.
questions.answer:
Data: Shuvo received full compensation and then asked for the 2 lakh BDT from the sale of the wreckage.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a full claim is settled, the ownership of the remaining property passes to the insurer.
Evaluation: If Shuvo received both, he would profit (27 lakh total for a 25 lakh loss).
Decision: The refusal is logical. Legally, the 2 lakh BDT belongs to the insurance company under the Principle of Subrogation to offset the compensation paid.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ratul executed two separate insurance contracts of 12 lakh BDT and 8 lakh BDT with 'Alpha Insurance Company' and 'Beta Insurance Company' respectively for his risky glass factory. When a loss of 10 lakh BDT occurred in an accident, Alpha Insurance Company compensated Mr. Ratul's claimed amount. Later, Alpha Insurance Company claimed a portion of the compensation from Beta Insurance Company.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Liability Insurance?
questions.answer: Liability Insurance is a type of insurance that provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Trust is the capital of an insurance contract—Explain.
questions.answer: It is called the capital of insurance because the contract is based on 'Utmost Good Faith' (Uberrimae Fidei). Since the insurer cannot see every detail of the risk, they rely on the insured's honest disclosure; any lie makes the contract worthless.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance contract did Mr. Ratul execute with the two insurance companies? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Ratul insured the same factory with two different companies (Alpha and Beta) for the same risk.
Analysis: When the same subject matter is insured with more than one insurer, it is called 'Double Insurance'.
Conclusion: Mr. Ratul executed a 'Double Insurance' contract.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of Alpha Insurance Company claiming compensation from Beta Insurance Company in light of the relevant principle.
questions.answer:
Data: Alpha paid the full 10 lakh loss and then asked Beta for their share.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Contribution', when double insurance exists, all insurers are liable to share the loss proportionately. The formula is: (Sum Insured with one Co. / Total Sum Insured) × Actual Loss.
Evaluation: Alpha paid more than its share (which was 6 lakh) to provide immediate relief to the insured. Now, they have the legal right to recover the excess from Beta.
Decision: The claim is logical and legally valid under the Principle of Contribution, ensuring that insurers share the burden fairly.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Rakib is the owner of a garment factory. He provides adequate salary and allowances to his workers. Thinking about the stability of the factory, he opened an insurance policy for the factory. He also opened an insurance policy for the workers. Due to this, the workers are working in the factory with enthusiasm.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the consideration or price paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the risk coverage provided by the insurance policy.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Insurance is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because its purpose is to compensate the insured for the actual loss suffered and restore them to their pre-loss financial position, without allowing any profit.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance did Mr. Rakib take for his factory? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Rakib took a policy for the "stability of the factory" buildings and assets.
Analysis: Insurance that covers physical business assets against risks like fire or theft is called Property or Fire Insurance.
Conclusion: He took 'Property/Fire Insurance' for the factory.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of opening an insurance policy for the workers of Mr. Rakib's factory.
questions.answer:
Data: Rakib insured his "workers," which boosted their "enthusiasm."
Analysis: Group Life Insurance or Workers' Compensation Insurance provides financial security to employees' families in case of death or injury.
Evaluation: This reduces the workers' mental stress regarding life's uncertainty, leading to higher productivity and loyalty.
Decision: It is highly logical. It acts as a non-financial motivator, ensures social security, and protects the employer from massive sudden liabilities in case of workplace accidents.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ahsan insured his personal car for twenty lakh BDT. The car was completely damaged in an accident. When Mr. Ahsan presented the claim, the insurance institution provided full compensation. Meanwhile, a businessman bought the damaged car for thirty thousand BDT. When Mr. Ahsan claimed the money from the sale of the car, the insurance institution refused to pay him that money.
fullCQData.meta: Jalalabad Cantt Public School & College, Sylhet · 2025

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Insurance?
questions.answer: Insurance is a legal contract where the insurer agrees to compensate the insured for specific losses in return for a premium.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What do you mean by insurable interest? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurable interest is the legal right to insure an object or life because the insured has a financial relationship with it and would suffer a monetary loss if it were damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Under which principle did Mr. Ahsan receive money from the insurance institution? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Ahsan received "full compensation" for the total damage to his car.
Analysis: The principle that ensures the insured is compensated only for the actual loss and not more is the 'Principle of Indemnity'.
Conclusion: He received the money under the 'Principle of Indemnity'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance institution not fulfilling Mr. Ahsan's latest claim.
questions.answer:
Data: Ahsan was fully compensated but then claimed the 30,000 BDT "sale money" of the wreckage.
Analysis: Under the 'Principle of Subrogation', once a full claim is paid, all legal rights and ownership of the remains (salvage) pass to the insurer.
Evaluation: If he kept both, he would profit from the accident, violating the core of insurance law.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Legally, the sale proceeds belong to the insurer to offset the compensation they paid to Mr. Ahsan.

fullCQData.stem: Raju rented a house and made it into a garment warehouse. He executed an insurance contract with an insurance company for his garments and valuable machinery. As the value of the garments and machinery was not mentioned correctly in the contract, the company cancelled it. When he tried to insure the building in his own name, the company refused.
fullCQData.meta: Jalalabad Cantt Public School & College, Sylhet · 2025

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a Premium?
questions.answer: An insurance premium is the specific amount of money paid by the insured to the insurer in exchange for the promise to provide financial coverage against a risk.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Insurance contract is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
questions.answer: It is called a contract of indemnity because its primary goal is to compensate the insured for the actual loss suffered and return them to their original financial position without allowing any profit.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Why was Mr. Raju's insurance contract cancelled in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The "value of the garments and machinery" was not mentioned correctly.
Analysis: The 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' requires both parties to disclose all material facts accurately. Proper valuation is essential for determining the premium and liability.
Conclusion: The contract was cancelled due to a breach of the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' through misrepresentation of value.

questions.label: d
questions.text: What is the reason for the insurance company's refusal of Mr. Raju's proposal? Analyze with logic.
questions.answer:
Data: Raju tried to insure the "building" (which he rented) in his "own name."
Analysis: To insure any property, one must have 'Insurable Interest,' meaning a legal financial stake where they benefit from its safety. Since Raju is a tenant and not the owner, he has no legal ownership of the building.
Evaluation: If the building is damaged, the financial loss of the structure falls on the owner, not the tenant.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Raju lacks 'Insurable Interest' in the building structure, making the proposal legally invalid for him.

fullCQData.stem: Monir learns from his friend about a type of business that provides financial assistance against various risks related to human life and property. Currently, this business is becoming popular among everyone. Moreover, the business is regulated by a powerful institution.
fullCQData.meta: Jalalabad Cantt Public School & College, Sylhet · 2025

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Moral Risk?
questions.answer: Moral risk refers to the possibility of a loss occurring due to the dishonesty, bad character, or intentional negligence of the insured person to gain from the insurance.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What do you mean by the Principle of Contribution? Explain.
questions.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a person has multiple insurance policies for the same property and risk, the insurers will share the actual loss proportionately so that the insured does not receive more than the actual loss.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which business is mentioned in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The business provides "financial assistance against risks related to life and property."
Analysis: The professional system of risk transfer where uncertainty is managed through a contract and premium is called Insurance.
Conclusion: The business mentioned is the 'Insurance Business'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the contribution of the regulatory institution in the development of the business mentioned in the stem.
questions.answer:
Data: The business is "regulated by a powerful institution."
Analysis: In Bangladesh, this is the 'Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority' (IDRA).
Evaluation: IDRA creates rules, protects policyholders, ensures companies have enough capital to pay claims, and resolves disputes.
Decision: The regulatory institution is the backbone of the industry; its contribution ensures public trust and market stability, which are essential for the growth of insurance in the country.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Reza insured his personal car for fifteen (15) lakh BDT. The car was suddenly and completely damaged in an accident. After presenting his claim, he received full compensation. Subsequently, the insurance company sold the damaged car for two (2) lakh BDT. When Mr. Reza claimed the money from the sale, the company refused to pay.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is an Insurance Contract?
questions.answer: An insurance contract is a legal agreement where an insurer agrees to provide financial protection or compensation against specific risks in exchange for a premium payment.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Explain the principle of Proximate Cause in insurance.
questions.answer: The principle of 'Proximate Cause' (Causa Proxima) states that the insurer is only liable to pay if the loss was directly and immediately caused by a peril that is specifically covered in the insurance policy.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of general insurance did Mr. Reza take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Reza insured his "personal car" for a specific value.
Analysis: Insurance that covers land vehicles against physical damage and liability is known as Motor or Automobile Insurance.
Conclusion: Mr. Reza took 'Motor Insurance' (a branch of General Insurance).

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of Mr. Reza's claim being rejected by the insurance company in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: Reza received the "full compensation" (15 lakh) and then claimed the "sale money" (2 lakh) of the scrap.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation,' once an insurer pays the full loss, they acquire legal ownership of the salvage (wreckage).
Evaluation: If Reza kept both, he would receive 17 lakh for a 15 lakh loss, violating the 'Principle of Indemnity.'
Decision: The rejection is logical and legally sound. Under the Principle of Subrogation, the 2 lakh BDT rightfully belongs to the insurer to minimize their net loss.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Asif is a motor parts manufacturer. He took a loan of 10 lakh BDT from a bank to purchase a new machine. Additionally, he insured the movable and immovable properties of his institution.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Liability Insurance?
questions.answer: Liability insurance is a type of insurance that provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Explain the Principle of Contribution in insurance.
questions.answer: The Principle of Contribution states that if a property is insured with multiple companies for the same risk, all insurers will share the loss proportionately so that the insured only receives the actual loss amount and does not profit.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of risk might Mr. Asif face due to taking the bank loan? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Asif took a "10 lakh BDT loan" to buy a machine.
Analysis: Taking a debt creates a fixed financial obligation. If the business fails or income drops, he may not be able to repay the interest and principal.
Conclusion: Mr. Asif is facing 'Financial Risk'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Can Mr. Asif insure all of his property in the stem? Analyze in light of insurance principles.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Asif wants to insure his "movable and immovable properties."
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Insurable Interest,' a person can insure any property in which they have a legal financial stake—meaning they benefit from its safety and suffer from its loss.
Evaluation: Since he is the owner of the motor parts manufacturing institution, he has a direct legal and financial interest in its assets.
Decision: Yes, Mr. Asif can insure all of his property because he possesses the necessary 'Insurable Interest' as the owner.

fullCQData.stem: Badhon Sea Transport Company carries goods using its own 3 ships and 2 ships rented through a contract with another company. When the company wanted to insure all 5 ships together, the insurance company agreed to insure only the 3 owned ships. Later, 1 insured ship was completely damaged in an accident, and Badhon Company applied for compensation. The insurance institution paid the full claim. After selling the wreckage of the recovered ship for 50,000 BDT, the company claimed that money as well. However, the insurer refused.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Fundamental Risk?
questions.answer: Fundamental risk is a type of risk that is impersonal in origin and affects a large group of people or the entire economy, such as war, natural disasters, or inflation.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Is it mandatory for an insurance contract to be in writing? Explain.
questions.answer: Yes, an insurance contract should be in writing because it serves as legal evidence (the policy) in case of disputes, ensures all terms and conditions are clearly defined, and is required by insurance laws for enforceability.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Due to the absence of which element of the insurance contract did the insurer not insure the other 2 ships? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The company tried to insure 2 "rented" ships.
Analysis: 'Insurable Interest' is a mandatory element where the insured must have a legal financial relationship with the subject matter. For a ship, this interest usually lies with the legal owner.
Conclusion: The insurer refused due to the absence of 'Insurable Interest' on the part of Badhon Company for the rented ships.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic behind the insurance institution not fulfilling Badhon Company's latest claim.
questions.answer:
Data: Badhon Company was fully compensated for the ship and then claimed the 50,000 BDT from the "wreckage sale."
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Subrogation,' once the insurer pays the full loss, the ownership of the salvage (remains) passes to the insurer.
Evaluation: If the company kept both, they would receive more than the actual loss value.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Legally, the 50,000 BDT belongs to the insurance company under the Principle of Subrogation to minimize their total loss.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shafiq invested all the money from his land sale in the stock market. Knowing there is risk, he went to an insurance company to insure his investment, but the company refused. On the other hand, Mr. Rafi took a 10-year life insurance policy in 2020 and paid premiums at a fixed monthly rate. Upon Mr. Rafi's death in 2024, his child claimed the money, and the officer advised him to submit a certificate.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Liability Insurance?
questions.answer: Liability Insurance provides protection against claims resulting from injuries and damage to people or property for which the insured is legally responsible.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What role does the regulatory body play in developing the insurance business in Bangladesh? Explain.
questions.answer: The 'Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority' (IDRA) ensures transparency, protects policyholders' rights, monitors company solvency, and sets guidelines to build public trust and stabilize the market.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which certificate did the insurance officer advise Mr. Rafi's child to submit? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: Mr. Rafi (the insured) died, and his child is claiming the death benefit.
Analysis: To process a life insurance claim, the company needs legal proof of the cause and fact of death.
Conclusion: The officer advised him to submit a 'Death Certificate'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance company's refusal of Mr. Shafiq's proposal.
questions.answer:
Data: Shafiq tried to insure "stock market investments."
Analysis: Insurance only covers 'Pure Risks' (where there is only a chance of loss or no loss). Stock market risk is a 'Speculative Risk' involving the chance of gain.
Evaluation: Speculative risks are generally uninsurable because they involve intentional profit-seeking.
Decision: The refusal is logical. Stock market volatility is not a standard insurable peril.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Sajal, a businessman, bought a Royal Enfield motorcycle for 3 lakh BDT in 2024 and gifted it to his elder brother. When the brother tried to insure it, the company refused. Later, Sajal insured it himself but mentioned the price as 4 lakh BDT. The bike was completely destroyed in an accident. After verification, the company refused to pay the claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Fundamental Risk?
questions.answer: Fundamental risk is a risk that affects the entire society or a large group of people simultaneously, such as natural disasters or national economic shifts.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is insurable interest important in the insurance business? Explain.
questions.answer: Insurable interest prevents insurance from becoming gambling by ensuring the insured has a legal financial stake in the subject, meaning they suffer a real loss if it's damaged.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Why couldn't Sajal's brother insure the motorcycle? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The brother tried to insure a bike that was "gifted" to him but likely not yet legally registered in his name.
Analysis: A person needs 'Insurable Interest' (legal ownership/stake) to insure property.
Conclusion: He couldn't insure it because he lacked legal 'Insurable Interest' at that moment.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logic of the insurance company's refusal to provide the insurance claim.
questions.answer:
Data: Sajal stated the price as "4 lakh" instead of the actual "3 lakh."
Analysis: This violates the 'Principle of Utmost Good Faith' (hiding/falsifying material facts).
Evaluation: Providing false information to get a higher claim is fraud.
Decision: The refusal is highly logical. The contract is void due to the breach of trust and misrepresentation of the asset's value.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ashik insured his personal car for 20 lakh BDT with two different companies separately. In an accident, the car suffered a loss of 5 lakh BDT. Mr. Ashik claimed 5 lakh BDT separately from both companies, but the companies rejected his claim.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Proximate Cause?
questions.answer: Proximate cause is the direct, immediate, and effective cause of a loss that is covered under the insurance policy.

questions.label: b
questions.text: An insurance contract is not a gambling contract—Explain.
questions.answer: Insurance is meant for protection and restoring the insured to their original position (Indemnity), whereas gambling is done to create a profit from chance.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What type of insurance policy did Mr. Ashik take in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The same car was insured with "two companies" for the same risk.
Analysis: Insuring the same subject with multiple insurers is known as 'Double Insurance'.
Conclusion: He took a 'Double Insurance' policy.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the reason behind the insurance companies' rejection of Mr. Ashik's claim.
questions.answer:
Data: Ashik claimed 5 lakh from *each* (total 10 lakh) for a 5 lakh loss.
Analysis: According to the 'Principle of Contribution' and 'Indemnity,' the insured cannot receive more than the actual loss.
Evaluation: Claiming double the loss amount is an attempt to profit, which is illegal in insurance.
Decision: Rejection was logical. The companies are only obligated to share the 5 lakh loss proportionately (e.g., 2.5 lakh each).

fullCQData.stem: Mrs. Rabeya owns a house in Uttara Housing. Her elder brother lives there with his family and looks after the house. When the brother went to an insurance institution to insure the house, they refused.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the full form of IDRA?
questions.answer: IDRA stands for Insurance Development and Regulatory Authority.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Insurance is a contract of indemnity—Explain.
questions.answer: It means the insurer promises to compensate the insured only for the actual amount of loss suffered, ensuring no financial gain from the accident.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Why couldn't Mrs. Rabeya's brother insure the house? Explain.
questions.answer:
Data: The "brother" tried to insure a house owned by "Mrs. Rabeya."
Analysis: Insurance requires 'Insurable Interest.' The brother is just a resident/caretaker; he has no legal ownership/financial stake in the building's value.
Conclusion: He was refused because he lacked 'Insurable Interest'.

questions.label: d
questions.text: How can the house in the stem be insured? Analyze your opinion.
questions.answer:
Data: The house needs insurance but the owner (Rabeya) isn't the one applying.
Analysis: For a valid contract, the person with the legal title must be the policyholder.
Evaluation: Rabeya can insure it herself, or she can give her brother 'Power of Attorney' or a legal stake to create insurable interest.
Decision: The most straightforward way is for Mrs. Rabeya, the legal owner, to apply for the insurance policy as she possesses the 'Insurable Interest'.

mcqData.text: Price paid for assuming risk in an insurance policy is called?
mcqData.explanation: The premium is the consideration paid by the insured to the insurer.

mcqData.text: Which insurance involves both policyholder and insurer as insurance companies?
mcqData.explanation: Re-insurance is an agreement where one insurance company transfers risk to another.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Nazif did a 10-year term policy. At maturity, he filed a claim. Q: What type of policy?
mcqData.explanation: A policy with a fixed duration (10 years) is an Endowment/Term policy.

mcqData.text:
Stem: (Same as above) Q: He will get the claim because:

(i) Co. error
(ii) Paid premiums
(iii) Indemnity rule doesn't apply
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance is not a contract of indemnity; he gets the sum assured because premiums were paid.

mcqData.text: Which is related to life insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance principles revolve around proximate cause; indemnity/subrogation are not applicable.

mcqData.text: Mortality table is?
mcqData.explanation: Mortality tables measure the probability of death per 1,000 individuals at specific ages.

mcqData.text: Mr. Abul stated age 2 years less to pay lower premiums. Which principle did he violate?
mcqData.explanation: Misrepresenting material facts (age) violates the principle of Utmost Good Faith.

mcqData.text: Which policy offers loan facilities?
mcqData.explanation: Whole life policies accumulate cash value, allowing for loans.

mcqData.text: Surrender value amount depends on?
mcqData.explanation: Surrender value is calculated based on premiums accumulated over time.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Jabbar took a 10-year policy but stopped after 4 years due to illness, and got some money back. Q: What is this money?
mcqData.explanation: The amount received for terminating a policy early is the Surrender Value.

mcqData.text:
Stem: (Same as above) Q: Characteristics of receiving this value:

(i) 2 years active
(ii) Portion of premium
(iii) Applicable to endowment/whole life
mcqData.explanation: All statements correctly describe the conditions for Surrender Value.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Rahman stated age 40 instead of 45 in his 10-year policy. Q: Which principle violated?
mcqData.explanation: Providing false information (age) is a breach of Utmost Good Faith.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: What should the company do?
mcqData.explanation: Material misrepresentation typically leads to the rejection of the claim.

mcqData.text: Price paid to insurer for risk assumption?
mcqData.explanation: The premium is the price of risk assumption.

mcqData.text: Which insurance provides a bonus?
mcqData.explanation: Only life insurance policies typically distribute bonuses from company profits.

mcqData.text: Which policy provides a bonus?
mcqData.explanation: Bonuses are specific to life insurance.

mcqData.text: Main objective of Double Insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Double/multiple insurance intends to reduce the impact of risk.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. P took a low-premium policy before going abroad. Q: What type?
mcqData.explanation: Term policies (সাময়িক বিমাপত্র) have the lowest premiums for specific periods.

mcqData.text:
Stem: (Same as above) Q: Why is the premium lower?

(i) Death payout only
(ii) Short period
(iii) Renewable
mcqData.explanation: Premiums are low because they are temporary and provide coverage only in the event of death during the term.

mcqData.text:
Premium determination factors:

(i) Age
(ii) Status
(iii) Sum assured
mcqData.explanation: Age and sum assured are mathematical factors; status (social) is not.

mcqData.text: Price paid for risk assumption?
mcqData.explanation: The premium is the price of risk.

mcqData.text: Which is called a "Contract of Assurance"?
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance is a "certainty" (assurance) as death is certain, whereas property is "uncertainty" (insurance).

mcqData.text: How does life insurance reduce inflation?
mcqData.explanation: By collecting savings and investing them, insurers help manage economic liquidity.

mcqData.text: Fundamental subject of Life Insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Human life is the fundamental subject matter.

mcqData.text:
Stem: Shubho wanted to insure his friend Dipon's life; rejected. Wife's life accepted with many risks. Q: Risks for wife's policy?

(i) Death
(ii) Maternity
(iii) Unemployment
mcqData.explanation: Life policies cover death and sometimes maternity, but not unemployment.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as above) Q: Why was friend's life rejected?
mcqData.explanation: One does not have an insurable interest in a friend's life.

mcqData.text: Price paid to insurer for risk?
mcqData.explanation: The premium is the consideration.

mcqData.text:
Re-insurance includes:

(i) Multiple insured
(ii) Multiple insurers
(iii) Single insured
mcqData.explanation: Re-insurance involves the original insurer (single insured party) transferring risk to one or more other insurers.

mcqData.text: Stem: Abir (pilot) took 12L for 3 years, claim rejected. Q: What policy?
mcqData.explanation: A temporary policy covers only the short term.

mcqData.text:
Stem: (Same as above) Q: Characteristics:

(i) Holder claims
(ii) Nominee gets
(iii) High-risk professions allowed
mcqData.explanation: In a temporary policy, the nominee gets the benefit if the insured dies during the term, and it's common for high-risk jobs.

mcqData.text: Which insurance provides an annuity?
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance often offers annuity (periodic payments) options.

mcqData.text: Life insurance is a contract of-
mcqData.explanation: Unlike property insurance, life insurance is not a contract of indemnity.

mcqData.text:
Essential element of Life Insurance:

(i) Insurable Interest
(ii) Good faith
(iii) Proximate cause
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance is not a contract of indemnity, so proximate cause does not apply in the same way as fire insurance.

mcqData.text: What is the return called when a policy is surrendered?
mcqData.explanation: This is the specific term for the cash value returned upon early cancellation.

mcqData.text: Consideration for risk assumption is?
mcqData.explanation: The premium is the price paid for the insurer to assume risk.

mcqData.text:
Life insurance claims are paid at:

(i) Death
(ii) Maturity
(iii) Other losses
mcqData.explanation: Life insurance does not cover "other losses" (like damage to items).

mcqData.text: Designating who receives money upon death?
mcqData.explanation: Nomination is the process of naming a beneficiary.

mcqData.text: Life insurance is a contract of-
mcqData.explanation: Because death is certain, it is a contract of assurance.

mcqData.text: Mortality table means?
mcqData.explanation: It is the statistical death rate per 1000 persons.

mcqData.text: In which insurance is a bonus paid?
mcqData.explanation: Bonuses are unique to participating life insurance policies.

mcqData.text: Which is a multi-life policy?
mcqData.explanation: Group insurance covers multiple lives under one master policy.

mcqData.text: Maximum duration of temporary insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Temporary/Term insurance is typically for one year or less, renewable.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mrs. Nishat wants a policy paying at maturity even if alive, higher premium. Q: What policy?
mcqData.explanation: Endowment policies pay at maturity or death and have higher premiums than term insurance.

mcqData.text:
Why is premium higher here?

(i) Savings/Investment
(ii) Loan
(iii) Guaranteed claim
mcqData.explanation: Endowment policies include savings and investment components, raising premiums.

mcqData.text: Which is a contract of assurance?
mcqData.explanation: Life is a contract of assurance.

mcqData.text: Getting more than expected is?
mcqData.explanation: This is the definition of an insurance bonus.

mcqData.text: Min. years to hold policy for surrender value?
mcqData.explanation: 2 years is the industry standard for acquiring surrender value.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Arif (shipyard owner) wants to insure all workers under one policy. Q: What insurance?
mcqData.explanation: Insuring many people under one policy is Group Insurance.

mcqData.text:
Benefits of this insurance:

(i) Low cost
(ii) Low premium
(iii) Saving/Investment
mcqData.explanation: Group insurance is cost-effective and has low premiums; it lacks individual investment features.

mcqData.text: Claim paid after death?
mcqData.explanation: Whole life insurance is designed primarily to pay out after death.

mcqData.text: Which insurance uses proportional compensation?
mcqData.explanation: Double insurance implies multiple policies; indemnity is shared proportionally.

mcqData.text:
Factors for premium:

(i) Age
(ii) Status
(iii) Policy value
mcqData.explanation: Age and policy value (sum assured) are critical; status is subjective.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. X took low-premium policy before travel. Q: What policy?
mcqData.explanation: Temporary/Term policies are for specific durations with lower premiums.

mcqData.text:
Why is premium low here?

(i) Death payout only
(ii) Short period
(iii) Renewable
mcqData.explanation: It is low because it is term-based and only pays out if death occurs within that time.

mcqData.text:
Why is Whole Life popular?

(i) Low premium
(ii) Easy payout
(iii) Bonus/Annuity
mcqData.explanation: It provides long-term coverage, bonuses, and investment benefits.

mcqData.text: Who gets money upon death?
mcqData.explanation: The nominee is the legally designated recipient.

mcqData.text: Essential component of Life Insurance contract?
mcqData.explanation: Insurable Interest is the universal legal pillar.

mcqData.text: Life Insurance feature?
mcqData.explanation: Insurable Interest is the special feature.

mcqData.text: Paid at death?
mcqData.explanation: Whole life pays on the death of the insured.

mcqData.text: What does Surma Insurance distribute to holders?

shortCQData.question: What is reinsurance?
